Figure 3.9. Open Loop Split Delegator-Service Model

‘The open loop model requires a verifiable data registry (VDR). This means that the service
‘may verify any authorization against an independent registry. The delegator registers the original
authorization in the registry. Registration by delegates with the VDR is optional. It depends on
whether or not there are additional verifications of the authenticity of the delegates or if the au-
thorization is a bearer authorization (any delegate will do). The major advantages of an open
loop authorization system are as follows: Data privacy with respect to correlation by the dele-
gator of the

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
behavior of the delegates may be better protected. Authenticity of delegates may be
independently verified. Authorization lifeeycle may be independent of delegator key rotation.
That is authorization may be persistent across rotations. This means that authorization do not
have to be reissued in the event of a key rotation.

3.3 Persistent vs. Ephemeral

In this case persistent means that authorizations may remain valid in spite of a change or rota-
tion of the authoritative keys used to sign the authorization. Ephemeral means that the authoriza-
tions are automatically revoked (ic. do not persist) across a key rotation. This is an important
Teature in large scale operations where key management infrastructure may be multi-valent and
authorizations may be hicrarchical. As deseribed in the scetion above one advantage of open-
loop systems is that they enable the de-coupling of authorization life-cycle events from key man-

1223

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
agement events such as key rotation while still preserving privacy and decentralization. While
closed loop models could employ such de-coupling they still suffer from the fact that they enable
correlation of the behavior of the delegates and tend to centralize key management and autho-
rizations. Different authorization revocation rules are described in more details here [34].
3.4 Chained vs. Unchained

The diagrams above show delegation of authorization which is a type of chained authorization
model. But some authorization models to not allow chaining. This is one of the limitations of the
current VC specification which has no standing chaining semantic. Chaining is essential for any
scalable provenancing. Besides the applications so far described in this paper for chaining se-
‘mantics, an important one is for secure attestation semantics. The IETF and TCG (Trusted Com-
puting Group) are promulgating several standards based on verifiable remote attestations of the
configuration of trusted exccutions environments [26; 38; 43]. The semantics of these verifiable
attestations could conveyed by ADCs (VCs) with appropriate chaining and provenancing
semantics
3.5 Attenuable vs. Unattenuable

‘The diagrams above show attenuation of authorization which is dependent on chained autho-
rization. Attenuation provides a practical trade-off between issuing many fine grained fit for pur-
pose authorizations and a few but too permissive general authorizations. A delegate who in fum
chooses to become a delegator may issuc a more limited fit-for-purpose delegation that is an at-
tenuation of a more general authorization it holds. This limits the risk to the delegator of the at-
tenuated  delegation while providing the flexibility to delegate. Most delegated authorization
‘mechanisms benefit from at least limited attenuation capabilities.
3.6 Aggregative vs. Unaggregative

In complex or rather practical real world decision making tasks (as opposed to trivialized ones)
aggregation of constraints is the usual situation rather than an exceptional case. Aggregation al-
Tows the joining of multiple authorizations for composite authorizations. When chained it allows
for trees of authorizations. With a tree authorization the vast majority of hierarchical decision
‘making structures may be implemented. It is notable that access control is not usually hicrarchi-
cally aggregated this makes most access control mechanisms including many implementation of
object capabilities ill suited

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
to real work decision making under authority constrains (i.¢. autho-
rizations). The following diagram shows an aggregation tree.

1323

content:
Figure 3.10. Aggregation Tree
The inverse of aggregation is expansion. In this case multiple delegates may be authorized by
a single delegator. The following diagram shows an expansion tree.

In systems where both aggregation and expansion are possible then complex relationships between
delegators and delegates with respect to authorization are possible. This is shown in the following
diagram.

Figure 3.12. Complex Authorization Relationships
3.7 Weighted vs. Unweighted

‘When aggregating authorizations (constraint satisfaction) richer aggregations arc provided by
relative weighting of the different authorizations being aggregated. This makes little sense in an
access control applications because access control control rules are Boolean. Either one gains ac-
cess or one does not. But in more complex decision making tasks, especially chained hierarchi-
cal constraints the end authorization may be evaluated as satisfied when the aggregate reaches a
threshold. In simple k of n threshold scheme all authorizations have the same weight. But more sophisti-
cated schemes benefit from different weights being assigned to different aggregated authorizations. One
example is the weighted signing thresholds in KERI [31].
3.8 Implicit vs. Explicit

Many credentials carry implicit authorizations. The actual actions that are authorized are not
cembedded or specified as rules or parameters or data embedded in the authorization itself but are
implied by the type or identity of the authorization. A drivers license is a good example. A dri-
vers license entitles the holder to driving privileges but those privileges are speeificd elsewhere
and there is no direct way to evaluate them upon presentation of the authorization. Indeed the
‘most common implementation of implicit authorizations are credentials and no doubt inspired
the use of the term verifiable credential. In contrast, object capabilities, access tokens and the
like are explicit authorizations with embedded are expressed rules for evaluating the associated
privileges. Many applications besides access control such as custodianship or guardianship or
consent benefit from explicit authorization semantics.

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
3.9 Cooperative vs. Uncooperative

A unique form of delegated authorization is provided by KERI for delegating identifiers [31;
34). This enables delegated authorization from a more secure key management infrastructure but
less performant to a less secure but more performant key management infrastructure while en-
abling recovery of key compromise to be protected by the more secure delegating infrastructure.
‘This enables horizontally scalable performance without losing ulfimate protection. In coopera-
tive delegation both the delegator and delegate must participate in the creation of the authoriza-
tion. It i not merely a one way authorization down from the delegator. The following diagram
shows KERI’s cooperative identifier delegation:

Figure 3.13. Cooperative Delogation in KERI
Another important use case for cooperative delegated authorization is consent [12; 12; 13; 19].
Several specifications have been developed around consentful authorization these include the
Kantara Initiatives Consent Receipt specification which is a nascent 1SO 27560 standard [13;
19]. Another consentful authorization is the consent to create binding specification which arc in-
cluded in the Kantara Distributed Assurance specifications [3; 12,
A consentful authorization imposes  liability on the delegate. The delegate must agree to the
liability as a condition of creating the authorization. This makes it a cooperative authorization
1523

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
Oftent a consentful authorization may involve a disclosure of confidential information. Consent
may thereby be used to protect privacy via the imposition of liability counter incentives to fur-
ther disclosure. Chained consent enables delegated liability. This has been called chain link con-
fidentiality [10]

There are two primary approaches to privacy protection in online exchanges of information.
+ Manage the degree of disclosure
+ Manage the degree of exploitation given disclosure

An open loop authorization model may employ both. Verification to the VDR not the issuer
mitigate upstream correlation and consentful cooperative authorization mitigate downstream
correlation.

‘The first approach is inherently leaky over time. Any disclosure over time becomes more and
more correlatable. Limited disclosure without consent liability protection provides ephemeral
privacy protection.

With cooperatively chained relationships, the chaining may be viewed as flowing both ways.
Authorizations o constraints on actions flows from delegator to delegate. Whereas credence or
trust flows from delegate to delegator. Likewise authority flows from delegator to delegate
Provenance is about tracing these relationships in cither direction depending on what is being
flowed. In each cooperative relationship we can think of the delegator as sending authority to
delegate and the delegate sending credence 1o the delegator.

From a decision making perspective, when verifying aggregative cooperative provenance rela-
tionships we can express the verification of those relationships with rules. Thus verifications
also becomes cooperative. For example the delegator imposes constraint rules for authorizations
in order to restrict actions by the delegate and the delegate evaluates composition rules for au-
thorizations in order Lo aggregate a set of authorizations to enable desired actions for meeting its
objectives. A verifier may have its own rules of both types (objectives and constraints) for decid-
ing whether or not 1o accept an authorization. A valid authorization therefore only occurs upon
the is the conjoint satisfaction of three sets of rules, namely: delegator, delegate and service. In
general authorization semantics include semantics for the expression and satisfaction of rule
chains. The DIF Presentation Exchange specification is an attempt to standardize the ways that
verifiers and holder presenting VC may better express and satisfy each other’s rules or require-
‘ments. It d

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
oes not include a chaining semantic but could be leveraged to provide a more expand-
ed specification for generic APCs [1]. Likewise the W3C-CCG Traceability Vocabulary Specifi-
cation provide some semantics for tracing the source of products in a supply chain [41]. It also
does ot have an explicit chaining semantic but could be leveraged to inform a more generic
APC semantic.

3.10 Object Capabilities vs. Verifiable Credentials

Given the list of authorization properties we can classify and compare typical object capabili-
ties and typical VCs as authorizations.

Typically object capabilities implementations such as ZCAP-LD provide closed-loop,
ephiemeral,  attenuable, chained, unaggregative, unweighted, explicit, uncooperative
authorizations.

Whereas the typical VC implementations provide open-loop, persistent, unattenuable, un-
chained, aggregative, unweighted, implicit, uncooperative authorizations.

As the foregoing dissuasion should clearly expose, other combinations of properties have merit
and therefore would benefit from support for expressing those other combinations in an inter-

1923

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
operable standard way. This is the goal of ADCs with APCs. What is also clear by this compari-
son is the ZCAP-LD is much too limiting as a general authorization specification and VCs arc a
better candidate.

4 ExamrLE Use Case

As a departure point a seminal use case is presented.

Steve is as a seller what like to sell an item to a buyer Bob would works for Edge Co. In order
to buy Bob needs authorizations from his employer. Steve as the seller needs to verify that Bob
has sufficient authorizations in order to trust that Steve will indeed by paid.

Bob is an employee of Edge Co who is authorized to make purchases. Edge Co is very large
and contracts with several identity proofers who issuc credentials of proof is identity. Edge Co
issues to each of these contracted identity proofers an authorization to be an authorized identity
proofer for Fdge Co. Therefore Steve must validate several credentials (authorizations). These
are one from Edge Co to Bob indicating that Bob is an Employee. One from Edge Co to any of
the identity proofers to authorize them as valid proofers for Edge Co employees and one from
one of the Identity Proofers to Bob indicating proof of Bob’s identity (name ec).

Edge Co authorizes employees to issue purchase orders with an authorizations from either the
director of procurement or by any two procurement managers. A procurement manger must
have a credential from the dircctor of procurcment authorizing the manager to delegate procure-
‘ment authorization.

Edge Co also constrains the size of any procurement from any given source that may be autho-
tized by a procurement cmployce. This authorization must stipulate the maximum amount and
the source. It must be issued by a procurement manager or by the procurement director. Autho-
tizations above the maximum but be authorized by the CFO of Edge Co.

Each employce of Edge Co at any level of responsibility must have proof of identity from one
of the contracted identity proofers and an employee authorization issued by Edge Co. Anything
issued by Edge Co is actually issued by at least two of the managing dircctors of Edge Co. Man-
aging Directors are authorized by the controllers of a special set of governing keys for Edge Co.
These keys are known through a common knowledge well know associations. These include an
credential from an industry legal entity vetting organization that certifies the root identiffer asso-
ciated with this special set of governing keys.

In order for Steve to verify t

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
hat indeed the purchase order is valid, Steve must verify a hierar-
chical tree of authorizations. But because all authorizations in the tree may be verified computa-
tionally, this complex authorization may happen in the fraction of second. Steve need merely
have established prior to the verification the root identifiers and controlling keys for Edge Co
and cach of its contracted identity proofers. Bob then may present the leaf authorization in the
tree and then Steve can walk the trec to verify in tun cach credential.

Edge Co has published and signed its schema and rules for authorizing purchases. Steve may
verify against this set of rules and schema.

It would highly impractical if not prohibitively so for Steve to support verification of purchase
orders from multiple companies like Edge Co without a standardized set of semantics for the
schema and the rules for verifying these complex authorizations. But this example is indeed co-
herent with real world constraints. This means that the current VC spec may not actually reduce
transactions costs over current manual methods of verification without augmenting the standard
to support chaining and richer authorization semantics.

2023

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
Whereas ZCAP-LD does not provide sufficient capability to support these sort of real world
examples (non-access control).
5 CoNCLUSION
The goal of this effort is to explicate the properties of a universal ACDC (authentic chained
data container) ACDC specificaion. This may be decomposed into an ADC (authentic data con-
tainer) standard with an APC (authentic provenance chain) semantic standard. And to specifical-
ly evaluate whether or not the W3C VC specification may be expanded to provide a concrete im-
plementation of ADCS with APCs. The advantage of such an approach is that only one set of
tooling and one infrastructure would be needed to support the authentic data economy.

WHY THIS MATTERS
Today, the Internet is probably best described as » network comprised of all interconnected entities, traditionally
referring to human users and computers. When we add connected entities and deviees in the so-called Tnternet of
Things (ToT), the number of addressable elements s in the tens of billions, with an estimate of 75 b connected
10T devices in 2025 [4]. Software services, such as algorithms and bots, further extend this universe of identifiable
entities,
The resulting combinatorics of possible connections betwoen any given sat of entities is an impossibly largo
mumber. Yet in today's user journeys or business environments, agents (whether human, machine, or software)
increasingly need to communicate, sccess or transact with a diverse group of these interconnected objects to
achiove their goals in both the digital and physical worlds. This requires a straightforward and ubiquitos method
to address, verify, and connect, these clements togother.
Deinition of ntitye Something that has a distinct and independent existence cither in tho real or the digital
world. Examples of an entity are:

= Living Organism

Human or object identities are stored in multiple centralised or federated systems such as government, ERP, loT,
o manufucturing systems. From the standpoint of eryptographic trush verification, each of these centralised

value chain has no method to independently verily credentials of a huma

Why it Matters for People
Today when entitics want. their identities to be confirmed they transfer information such as a birth cortficate,
physical address, or social security mumber to multiple third parties, who start to validate the same data in
different contexts for KYC and authentication processes.
“The parties to which they sent that information retains it, meaning the data is out there in silos, creating risks in
terms of data loss, privacy broaches, and use of inconsistont data and forcing companics that might not want to
bein that position to store that information. Tt also enables businesses to harvest people's personal data for

‘This situation results in big problems for hurmans such as broken health care records. Patients will need a
universally addressable healthcare record system that is controlled by the patient itself, that consistently stores all
relevant verified health care data, and that is able to share this data with doctors that need to connect with it. To
enable tho doctors or algorithms, they need a data-flow provenanco to verify the integrity, quality, or roputation
of a healtheare record to decide on treatments and give the patient confidence abont the proposed treatments.
Why it Matters for Businesses
Deinition of  Digital Twin: A digital twin is a digital representation of either a real-world or digital entity. A
digital twin exists over the life-cycle of an entity from planing, manufacturing, testing, birth, and operations to
decommissioning and rense. The more past and present data. are related and anlysed, the moro knowledgo can be
deployed to drive significant improvements on an individual entity or system level.

a year in asset maintenance |5

Why it Matters for Objects, Machines and Agents

There is no widely adopted anthentication or verification systems in place to provide the equivalent of KYC
(know-yonr-customer) for non-human entities, that is: KYA (know-your-agent), KYB (know-your-bot), KYM
(know-your-machine}, or KYO (know-your-object).

Tn a world when objocts and machines are connected with datastroams and intelligent agents that perform

Digital twins of 3D-printed objects for safely eritical parts such as  turbine of an airplane provide an important
example. For these parts, it is important to have an precise andit trail about the 31 printing procass to prove
that the object was manufactured in accordance to stringent; specifications. The digital twin stores design.

Why it Matters for the World

The diverse application of decentralized identificrs (DIDs) will have substantial influcnces in broader applications

The promise of a overarching prevalence through the broad nse of DIDs also provides the key component, for

CCOMPREHENSIVE SOLUTION
Motivation
The Decentralized Autonomic Data. paper from the RWOT Spring 2018 introduced three new concepts related to
DIDs [1]

* Decentralized Autonomic Data (DADI) itemns

- Derived DID (dDID)

- Self.contained verification of data governance and integrity
This paper extends these concepts with a new one, that is

* Data flows may be provenanced via a selF-referential blockehain (Tist of signed DAD items where each

subsoquent DA item ineludes the DI or dDID and associated signatnre of the provious item)

Data Provenance
The concapts above lay the groundwork for dafasflow provenance that.is the focus of this paper. Tn this context
provenance is used in o gencral sense. By data-flow provenance we mean a mechanism for tracing data-item
content and control through a processing system, including any transformations to the data item or its
governance. This includes flows with multiple sources and sinlks of dat, independently and in combination. To
restate, data-flow provenance means not. just tracing control but, also verifying the end-to-end integrity of every
data flow, including any transformations (additions, deletions, modifications, and combinations).
In this context provenance refers fo the chain-of-custody of data items not ownership. Ownership s a more
complex idea than mere control. Change of ovnership requires the previous owner to relinquish ownership und the
new owner to accept ownership. Tt reverts back f not accepted. Finally, ownership implies . restriction or
limitation on rights %0 it may need to be interactive or involve & third party.
In English we use the term " custody’” to refer to having possession of something (under you control), but custody
does not equal ownership. With

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
physical objects one can have unique custody that could be equivalent to
ownership because a physical object is a unique thing, Hence the expression "possession is nine-tenths of the law"
07719 A DID for Everything 1.0 5

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
[17]. Because data can be casily duplicated, however, it may not be possible to prove mique possession of data.
There is 10 way to guarantee that there is not another copy of the data. So custody of data s not equivalent to
restricted ownership. With data the strongest attestation of ownership is limited to merely rights to use the dato.
So transfer of ownership of data is & difforent concept: i's not ransfer of the data itsclf but the transfer of rights
to use the data.
An important insight from tho viewpoint of a distributed application is that an entity's influenco on the
application is solly based on the digital data flows that move betwoen the entity and the other components of the
distributed application. We choose to call those dati flows the entity's projection onto the distributed application.
If those projections cousist of DADs and every interaetion of internal components consists of DADs then we have
& universal approach for implementing decentralized applications with total provenance of control and data. within
the application.
A major motivation for establishing data-flow provenance is to support building and architecting decentralizod
Processing systems that use a zero-trust or more correctly diffuse-frust perimeterless security model. The emerging
decentralized processing peradigun for distributed upplications where the sources and sinks of data may be
controlled by nuultiple entities, i.e. decentralized governance, means that traditional perimeter security models are
at best, problematic. Tndeed, even the newer perimetorloss scourity model as originally formulated assumes singular
governance of the network hosts and associated data [7][§]. We extend that model herein to include hosts and
DADs with decentralized governance vis DIDs,
An carlier paper explored the architectural issues of building distributed computing infrastructure that applied the
zero-trust security model but extended it to use distributed consensus for policy governance [9]. This type of
architecture wo ealled Zero-Trusi-Computing or more correctly Diffuse-Trust-Computing. A simple way of
explaining the 7ero-trust seenrity model is the mantra, "never trust, always verify". The paradigm of Zero Trust
Networking was first popularized in 2013 by & NIST report [7]. More recently the principles have received much
broader attention including the book Zero Trust Networks |8]. The busic approach to Diffuse-Trust-Computing is
o use a diffuse trust. perimeterloss seourity approach

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
. As previously mentioned, some call this a trustless or zero-
trust security model but that s a misnomer. There is still trust, it is just diffused in such a way that socurity is
greatly enhanced [3]. In this paper we further extend that model to use DIDs and DADs to provide decentralized
end-to-end data provenunce.
The caveats of conventional diffuse-trust perimeterless security are:

* The network is always hostile both internally and externally; locality is not trustorthy.

* By defuult, every network interaction or data flow must be authenticated and authorized usiug best

practices cryplography.
= By default, inter-host communication must be end-to-ond signed enerypted, and data must be stored
signed fencryped using best practices cryptography: Data is signed fencrypted at motion and af rest.

07719 A DID for Everything 1.0 6

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
- Policics for authentication and authorization must be dynamically modified based on behavior
(reputation)
Using end-to-end encryption and storage prevents exploits from anyone that merely has access to the network or
the data-storage device. By authenticating and authorizing every network interaction or dat flow, security
becomes granular. A successful exploit of onc interaction docs not. biced into any other. Compromising one daia
Hlow does not compromise any other. Escalation opportunities are minimized. Many security exploits are
discovered through repeated probes and experiments to find bugs, buffer overflows, o weaknesses in network
protocols or softsare implementations. Dynamie policy modification that uses AT to first profile and detect
anomalous behavior and then restrict the authorization of that usor prevents discovery. This adds time as a
defence.
Extending the model to enhance the sceurity of the policy management adds the following caveat
* Policies must be governed by distributed consensus.
Distributed consensus diffuses the trust for any policy decision to a group of hosts. In order to defeat the policy,
an attacker nust exploit some majority of the hosts. This makes exploits exponentially more dificult, Distributed
consensus also allows for decentralized governance of the hosts.
Ihis paper extends these priaciples with one more:
« By dofanlt, cach data flow including transformations must bo ond-to-cnd provenanced using decentralized
identifiers (DIDs) and hence decentralized autonomic data items (DADs)
This additional principle allows governance over the data using a decentralized trust model and a decentralized
web of trust based on DIDs and DADis. This approach enables truly decentralized governance models for
distributed applications. One can combine the third and sixth caveais from ahove, to a simple summary caver:
* By defuult, data flows are end-to-end provenanced /sigued/encrypted at motion and at rest using DIDs
and DADs.
“This remainder of this section in the paper will explore salient issues in using DIDs and DADs to maintain
provenance over each step in a data processing flow including transformations of the data 10 enable credible uses
of the data for various applications while maintaining a 7ero or difuse-trust security moel.
Decentralized Autonomic Data
Definition
First introduced in more detail here [1], & decentralized autonomic data item (DADI) is associated with o
decentralized identifier [14]. This paper does not provid

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
e a detailed definition of DIDs but does describe how DIDs
are used by a DADL. The DID syntax specification is a modification of standard URL syntax per RFC-3086 [19]
0719 ADID for Exerything 1.0 7

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
s such, it bonefits from familiarity, which is a boon to adoption. Onc of the featurcs of a DID s that it is a self
certifying identifier in that a DID includes cither a public key or a fingerprint of a public key from a cryptographic
public/private key puir. Thereby « signature created with the private key can be verified using the public key
provided by the DID. The inclusion of the public part of & crypiographic key pair in the DID gives the DID other
desirable propertics. These include universal uniquencss and psenodnynmity. Another way to describe a DID is
that it is a cryptonym: a cryptogeaphically derived psendonym.
Tn the DAD acronyin, decentraized means that the governance of the data may not reside with a single party.
The term autonomic means self-managing or self-regulating. Tn the context of data, we erystalize the meaning of
self-managing to include cryptographic techniques for maintaining data provenance that make the data self-
identifying, self-certifying, and self securing. Implied thereby is the use of cryptographic keys and signatures to
provide a root of trust for data integrity and to maintain that trust over change-in-custody and ransforsaation of
that data, i.o. provenance
The motivating use of DAD is to provide provenance for streaming data that is gonerated and processed in a
distribnted manner with decentralized governance, Stroaming data are typically measurements that are collocted
and agaregated to form higher level constructs. Applications include analytics and instrumentation of distributed
web or internet-of-things (IoT) applications as well as portable reputation systems. A DAD seeks to maintain &
provenance chain for data undergoing various processing siages that follows diffuse trust security principles
inclnding signed at rest and in motion.
Streaming data applications may impose significant. porformance demands on the processing of the associated
data.
Associated with  DID is a DID Document (DDO) [14]. The DDO provides meta-data abont the DID that can be
used to manage the DID as well as discover services affiliated with the DID. Typically the DDO is provided by a
DID resolution service, The DID/DDO model is not & good mateh for streaming data especially if & new
DID/DDO pair seould need to be created for each new DAD item. But a DID/DDO is a good match when used as
the root or master identifier from which an identificr for the DAD is derived. This derived identifier is called a
derived-DID or dDID. Thus only one DID/D

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
DO paring is required to manage a large number of DADs where
each DAD may have a unique dDID. The syntes for & dDID is identical for a DID. The difference is that ouly one
DDO with meta-data is needed for the root. DID used o create the dDIDs in the DADs. Each DAD item carries
any additional DAD-specific meta-data, thus making them self-contained (antonomic).
DID Syt
A DID or dDID has the following required syntax:

did:method: idstring
The method is some short string that namespaces the DID and provides for unique behavior in the associated
07719 A DID for Everything 1.0 8

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
method specification. Tn this paper we will use the method dad.
The idstring must be universally nique. The idstring can have multiple colon ":" separated parts, thus allowing
for namespacing, In this document the first part of the idstring s linked to the public member of a cryplographic
Key pair that is defined by the method. We wil use & At-character Bascs4 URT-File safe encoding as per RFC-
4648 [18], with onc trailing pad byte of the 32-byto public verification key for an EADSA (EA25519) signing key
pair. Unless otherwise specified Base64 in this document refers 1o the URL-File safe version of Base64, The URL-
Fille safe version of Base64 encoding replaces pins " " with minus =" and slash " /" with underscore " %. 4 DID
may have optional parts including a path, query, or fragment. These use the some syntaz as a URL, that is, the
path is delimited with slashes, /, the query with o question mark, 7, and the fragment with a pound sign, _
When the path part is provided then the query applies o the resource referenced by the path and the fragment
Tefers to an clement in the docnment,referencod by the path. An example follows:
did:dad:Xq5¥qal.6L48pE0 £ IUNLOTRAU2_RXFEOALA3wYn148=/mom?who=ne#blue
In contrast, when the path part s missing but either the query or fragment part is provided then the query
and/or fragment parts have special meaning. A query without a path means the the query is an operation on
cither the DID itsclf or the DID document (DDO). Likewise when a fragment is provided then the fragment is
referencing an element of the DDO. An example of a DID without a path but with a query follows:

did:dad: Xq5¥qal6L48pE0FuTIVNLOTRAUZ_RXFPOALA3wYn148=7who=me

s will be described later, a query part on a DID expression without a path pat will enable the generation of
dADIDs (derived DID).

DIDs form & new elass of identifiers that can be used to replace the identifiers commonly used in distributed
applications. DIDs can replace UUIDs, URTs, and Publie Keys because a DI combines the desirable features of
all three identifier classes.

- UUID: A Univorsally Unique dentifier per RFC 4122 has types 1-5. Those are 16-byte collision-resistant.
decentralized identifiers generated with  psendo-random-number generator and optional name-spacing
dats. A UUID ensbles distributed applications to create unique identifiers without central authority.
Profixed name-spacing allows for sorting and searching propertics such as time order, lesical order, nesting
cte.

* UR

author: SmithSamuelM

Minimal DAD
A minimal DAD (decentralized antonomic data) item is a data item that contains a DID or dDID that helps
uniquely identify that data item or affliated data stream. In this paper JSON s used to represent serialized DAD
itemns but other formats could be used instead. To ensure non-repudiable data integity (ic. that the data has not
been tampered with and can be assigned to a given signing entity) a signature is appended to the DAD item that
s verifiable as being generated by the private key associated with the public key in the i field value. This
signature verifies that the DAD item was created by the holder of the associated private key. The DAD item is
thereby both self-identifying and self-certifying because the identifier value given by the id field is included in the
sigmed data.and is verifiable against the private key associated with the public key obiained from the associaterd
DID in the id field. The signature is separated from the JSON serialization with characters that may not appear
in the JSON. An example DAD with & payload follows:
[
"id": "did:dad:Xq5YqaL6L4BpEO£uTIURLOJRAU2_ReFPOALA3WYn148=",
"data":
i
"name": "John Smith",
"nation": "USA"
)
}

Key-derivation algorithm. A dorived key is expressod as a branch in a tree of parent,fchild keys. Fach public key

Change Detection
Stale DAD items must ofien be detectable to provent replay attacks. A later re-transmission of an old copy of the
DAD item must not: supercede a newer copy. Using a sequence mumber or some other identifier could provide
change detection. Another way to provide change detection is for the DAD item to include & changed field whose
value is monotonically increasing and changes every time the data is changed. The source of the data can enforce
that the changed field value is monotonically inereasing. Typical approaches include a monotonically ineroasing
date-time stamp or soquence mumber. Any older data. items resent or replayed wonld have older date-time stamps
or lower sequence numbers and would thus be detectable s stele.
Below s an example of an non-trivial data item that has a changed field for change detection.
[

Change detection prevents replay attacks in the following manner. A second party reccives DAD updates that are
each signed by the associated private key. Each update has a monotonically increasing changed field. The source
sigrer controls the contents of the data wrapped by the signature, Therefore the signer controls any changed feld,
A consistent signer will use & monotonically increasing changed value whenever the data wrapped by the signature
s changed. Thus & malicious third party cannot replay earler instances of the DAD wrapped by & valid signature
o the original second party because the socond party knows to discard any receptions that have older changed
fields than the latest one they have already reccived.
On the Fiy dDIDs in DADs
One important use case for dDIDs in DADs is to identify data that is received from a source that is not providing
identifying information wits the data. The receiver then creates an sssociated DID and dDIDs to identify the
data. At some later point the receiver may be able to link this data with some other identifying information or the
source may "claim” this data by supplying identifying information. Tn this case the dDIDs are private to the
receiver but can later be used to credibly provenance the internal use of the data. This may be

beneficial when shared amongst the entitios in the processing chain as a way to manage the entailed proliferation
of keys that may all be claimed later as a hierarchical group. The DIDs and associated derivation operations for
dADIDS may be shared amongst » group of more-or-less trusted entities that are involved in the processing chain.
Data-uige dDIDs
Every DAD MUST have a unique DID. A databse of DADs could be addressable by either their DID or their
signature as for content-addressability. It might happen that an entity accidentally uses a public private key pair
1o create a dDID aud use it two or more times. One way Lo ensure uniqueness is to wse a random number as
UUID or a timestamp and concatenate it s an exension in the DID path

did:dad:QE27£ThHONZsaBEY Tkep6H-HABEr54SHONI VW1 6FE=/UUID
Auother way would be to use the signature in a content-addressable database to resolve the DAD.
Pubiic Deriuntion
Auother important use case for dDIDS in DADS is to avoid storing even the dDID with its derivation chain. This
may be an issue when a client wishes to communicate with a potentially very large mmber of public services.
Ench public service would be a new pairing with a wnique dDID. I the derivation algorithm for an HD-Key dDID
could use the public key or public DID of the public service to generate the dDID then the client need not store
the actual dDID but can recover the dDID by using the public DID of the server to re-derive the associated dDID.
This can be done by creating a hash of the root, DID private key and the remoto server public DID to create the
seed used to generate the dDID for the DAD. This also means that the dDIDs or chain codes do not have to be
included in the keys preserved by a key-recovery system.
Chaining DADs
The provenance of data in & data. flow through a data processing system with transformation can be established
by forming a lteral block chain of the data. When using DAD items to represent the data, the ehain of DADs can
be represented simply in  sclf-contained manner. At each step in the data flow of the originating DAD, where the
contained data s transformed in any way, a new DAD is generated by the controlling entity of the
transformation, This entity assigns a new DID (or dDID) to this DAD, The new DAD payload includes the DID
of the DAD prior to transformation as well as the signature of the prior DAD. This links the new DAD to the
prior DAD. The signature of the prior DAD provides both a hash that establishes the con

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
tent integrity of the
prior DAD as well a non-repudiation of the controller of the embedded prior DID. All the DADs in the data flow
need o be stored some place indexed by their DIDs. Given this storage, any single DAD can then be used to
recall the string of prior DADs back to the originating DAD or DADs. A special case is when an entity merely
wishes to establish custody of data without changing or transforming it. The simplest way to to this that the
entity adds a copy of the DAD as a link in the DAD chain without changing the underlying data. This forms an
assertion that. they control that link. IF they do not transform the data then merely signing is cnough to assrt
control over the link or equivalenty custody of the the data for that link of the chain. A one to many data stream
s just  branch or fork in the chain into multiple chains.

USE CASES

General

When working with people, the proposed system could use proximity verification, implemented with software
toolkits such as Google Nearby and p2pkit, to bind virtual identities to real people. This could be useful in many
situations. For example, if two people met over Craigslist t0 sell a used bicyele, then the system could verify that,
the to parties actnally met and transacted. Tf two people found cach other through a dating site, and followod np
with & face to face mecting, then the system could verify that these people were actually in the stated city and
not catfishers from Ukraine or Nigeria. Sinilarly, transactional events could build trust in & similar manner for
‘machines. For example, a cortifiod mechanic performing a tuno up on & car with a digital twin can also provide an
attestation for the DID for that antomabile. A complete history of the automobile's maintenance wonld add valne
for its owner

Tt should be noted that a single instance of meeting is not as trustable as an entire history of meoting many
people. For a state actor gencrating a legend for a sockpuppet, this would entail an unattainable level of work to
prove personhood. For a regular human being, it's relatively effortless to use the system in an organic and
unobtrusive manner. Furthermore, these histories of meetings and verifications — using location data to prove it
was not.in Nigoria, and time data to prove it wasn't in the middle of the night — would bo agaregated to increase
the trustability of the personhood assessment.

Every resource that is reference by a URL conld instead be referenced by a DID or dDID,

Use Case: Medical Imaging Workflow
Timage processing is operational in many sientific and engineering disciplines. These include:

- Tmage processing methods are used to count, measure, inspoet, and analyse objects, coded information,

and/or visnal patterns.
* Xeray and ultrasonic devices deliver medical images with image processing that the doctor or medical
algorithm can interpret more easily.

= Xeray machines in security zones automatically inspect lnggage and clothing for dangerous objocts.

- Visual image processing for quality assurance of abjeets in manufactring systems.

* Radar and visual images for earth-observation applications.
Image processing can include multiple data sources, parties, slgorithms, and processing steps. An imuge processing
end-user needs to be able to validate trustability and accuracy of an image data chain output data. This
requirements becomes of critical importance when the output data is nsed in safoty or socurity-rolevant use cases
or to make economic decision with significant commercial values involved.
The cryptogeaphic DAD data structures enable a user to validate the provenance of an image processing data
chain including the authenticity and integrity of the input data and the provenance of the processing algorithms,
We recormimend the ereation of DAD verifiable data chains for image processing that provide s DID for every
image and data processing output data set. These data. chains are then integrated with DID registries and /or
digital twins that provide information about organisations, imaging devices, external data sources, and software
algorithms involved in the data chain.
This approach is of partieular value when validation or benchmarking data are available about the image devices
and the algorithms that, azc proccssing the images. Tn combination with a reputation or validation system any user
can caleulate trustability and acenracy metrics about the output data.
Tn context of the medical image processing use case, the digital Lins of the imaging device include verifiable
claims issued by the OEM about, the anthenticity, accuracy, and calibration of th camera as well as
benchmarking information abont the accuracy of the machine learning algorithms. The followin

Use Case: Proof of Personhood
The root use case is to help us identify and manage our identity interactions with other people. But to do so more
effectively, we need to know that an identifier is accurately associated with an actual person. The fraudulent
‘misuse of identity has a significant negative fmpact on socicty. At an individual Tovel, frandulent users conld cheat
others in commercial transaction, become casfishers on dating sites, or worse. At a sacietal level, the fraudulent
‘misuse of identity through bots and sockpuppets have caused havoc in elections and through the manipulation of
public opinion through weaponized propaganda.
The highest purpose of the blockchain is as a kind of "truth machine". For decentralized identity to succeed, and
not be co-opted as just another way to empower sockpuppets and botnets, there needs to be un equivalent to
proof-of-work, a mechanism that binds physical entities to virtual identities in a way that enables accountability
while prescrving anonymity. This is now being referred to as "proaf of persanhoo"
One use case would be to use proxinity verification, mplemented with softare toolkits such as Google Nearby
and p2pki, to bind virtual identitics to rcal peaple, in 2. way that prescrves privacy, non-corrclation, 7ero-
Inowledge proofs, and pseudonymons operations. We're currently building a technology called Not_a_Bot, which
provides proof of personhood through a variety of techniques. One technique is to verify that the user has actually
met another actual person, in physical space... and is not a catfisher, not a scammer, and "not a bot"
1t shonld be noted that a single instance of meeting is not as trustable as an entire history of meeting many
people. For a state actor generating a legend for a sockpuppet, this would entail an unattainable level of work to
prove personhood. For a regular human being, it's relatively effortless 1o use the system in an organic and

Onee a oot personhood verification could be insnred, then trustable psendonyms conld be generated. Adding this
verification to DIDs would provide trust in a trustless environment, as the DID could then provide identity and
credentialing services in environments that support, or even require, pseudonymity.
Decentralized Fog Computing Infrastructure
The coming tidal wave of digital data due to the proliferation of digital devices will require an exponential
increase in computing capacity for data integration and analysis. Currently data integration and analysis is
handled predominantly in the elond. This is not. efficient. as it requires transport of data from the edge of the
internet where it is created to remote data centers that are the clond for processing and then transport back fo
the edge of the results of processing. In many cases the data processing can be performed in a hierarchical-tree-like
bottorm-up fashion which is more cost effectively done in the ede close to the data. Likewise new applications
benefit from low latency processing of data near the sources and sinks of the data. These applications also bencfit
from processing the data in the edge. Currently there is lttle capacity to perform processing in the edge.
Consequently an opportunity exists to build a public decontralized odge computing infrastructure. Edge
computing infrastructure is called the fog. A public decentralized fog computing infrastructure provides an
opportunity to scale capacity using two sided network effects where a large mumber of entities can cooperatively
participate in the two-sided network both as consumers and as inportantly producers of compute. This is in
contrast to the highly contralizod nature of clond computing shere a handful of producers control most of the
public computing infrastructure, This stifies innovation and raises costs. A public, decentralized, fog computing
infrustructure could enable the monetization of pre-existing spure capacity in the form of bandwidth, space,
energy, and compute devices. This would provide lower costs and incentivize innovation.
FUTURE DIRECTION
Data flows can be provenanced by verifying the end-to-cnd integrity of data. with DIDs. By enabling DIDs to sign
claims about other DIDs, the fidelity of these data ows can be increased further, There sre several ideas that are
ood candidates for future exploration. These are as follows

7. Examine how attestations or other types of claims on a DID can build an airibution graph that incrcases

CONCLUSION
Tmagine o world whero this proposed technology has been deployed and globally adopted. Let us paint a picture
for how this might be achicved. Tmagine that this approach becomes part of a decentralized identity solution for
every entity, driven by a robust and active developer community. The vision is to generate technologies that
would be integrated into applications that are used in 0T, e-commerce, social interaction, banking, healthare,
and 50 on. Now imagine that mabile tolephony companies agree to embed the technology into the operating
systems for all smartphones, and the dominant social network providers agree to nse DIDs and DADs and proofs
about the entities controling these DIDs and DADs in their algorithms for determining which content to propel.
This would mean the end of phishing, The end of fuke news. This is the begiuning of new era for society, built on
an interconnecting web of trust: a world in which we know what impacts we are having. The emergent, property of
this now data fabric is Knowing,
This is greatly needed as trust in media is at an all-time low, and centralized, algorithmic distribution have
ereated a perfect: storm for the rise of misinformation, disinformation, and fake news. This is driving polarization
while sinultancously undermining public trust in institutions,
However, realistically, most of society's greatest challenges have no silver-bullet solution. Consider the problem of
using sock puppets to weaponize propaganda. Proximity verificution is one component of a multi-pronged solution
that might lielp mitigate the problem. Consider that, certain highly problematic diseases can be treated with drug,
combinations consisting of antiretroviral compounds mixed with ranscriptase inhibitors and steroids. The
combinations are called "cocktails," and they're so cffective that they're called the "Lazarus Effect," named for
the biblical figure who was raised from the dead. Cocktails can turn an HIV death sentence into & manageable
chronic condition,

Preventing the weaponization of that propaganda is the other half, and this proposed technique could help
provide at least part of a comprehensive cocklail prescription to address the issue of fake news

The Internet's current capacity Lo support democratic societies in making well-informed decisions is being
subverted by globally networked state actors. However, there are additional benefits for this tochnology in
computing, social networking, connected governmental servicos, and e-commeree — where the use of sockpuppets
is more of an aggravation than a grave danger. For exumple, in terms of government service, we envision a system
where elected offcials could verify how many people they actually meet and how much time was spent with them,
1o back up claims of being  "man of the people". For fully transparent, politics, this system should could provide
the eloctorate with an accurate sense of whether a politician has actually met with leaders of social movements or
is spending the majority of time with donors, lobbyists, and political action committees,

Underlying the benefits of decentralized identity outlined above is the need for open interoperable standards to
ensure the reputable provenance of the associated data flows between decentralized entities. This paper describes a
novel concept.for provenancing data flows sing DADis (Decentralized Autonomic Data items) that are built upon
the emerging DID standard. This approach uses and extends the advanced diffuse-trust or zero-trust compnting
paradigm that is needed to operate securely in a world of decentralized data.

Importantly this paper provides detailed descriptions of the minimally sufficient means for key reproduction,
rotation, and recovery for DID leveraged DADS.
OVERVIEW
A decontralized autanomie data (DAD) item is assaciated with a decentralized idontificr, (DID). This paper docs
not.provided a detailed definition of DIDs but docs deseribe how DIDs are used by a DAD. The DID syntax
specification is a modification of standard URL syntax per REC 3986 As such, it benofits from familiaricy, which
s a boon to adoption. Onc of the features of a DID is that it i a slf ccrtifying identifier in that a DID includes
cither a public key or a fingerprint of & public key from a cryptographic public/private ke pair. Therehy a
signature ereated with the private key can be verified using the public key provided by the DID. Tho inclusion of
the public part of a cyplographic key pair in the DID give the DID other desirable propertics. These include
miversal uniquencss and pseuodnynmity. Because a cryptographic key pair is generated from  large random
mumber there is an infintessimal chance that any two DIDs are the same (collision resistance). Another way to
deseribe a DID s that it is a cryplonyin, a cryptographically derived pseudonym.
Associated with a DID is a DID Document (DDO). The DDO provides meta-data about the DID that can be used
o manago the DID as well as discover services affiliatod with the DID. Typically the DDO is meant to be
provided by some service. The DID/DDO model is not & good maich for streaming data especially if a new
DID/DDO pair would need to be crcated for cach new DAD item. But a DID/DDO is a good match when usod as
the root or master identifier from which an identificr for the DAD is derived. This derived identifier is called a
derived DID or DDID. Thus only one DID/DDO paring s required to manage a large number of DADs where
each DAD may have a unique DDID. The syntax for a DDID is identical for a DID. The difference is that only
one DDO with meta-data is needed for the root, DI and all the DAD items earry any additional DAD-specific
‘meta-data, this making them self-contained (autonomic).
DID Syntax
A DID or DDID has the following roquired syntax

did:method: 1dstring
The method is some short string that namespaces the DID and provides for unique behavior in the associated
method specification. In this paper we will use the method dad.
The idstring rmust be

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
universally unique. The idstring can have multiple colon *" separated ports, thus allowing
for namespacing. In this document the frst part of the idstring is linked to the public member of a cryplographic
Key pair that is defined by the method. In this paper we will use  44-character Base64 URL-File safe encoding as
per REC-4645, with one trailing pad byte of the 32-byte public verification key for an EADSA (Ed23519) signing
Key pair. Unless otherwise specified Base6d in this document refers to the URL-File safe version of Base64. The
URL-File safe version of Base64 encoding replaces plus *+" with minus *” and slash "1 with underscore *_"
As an example a DID using this formiat would be as follows;

did:dad:Xq5YqaL6L4BpFOuTIURLOJRAU2_RxFROALA3wWYn148=
Au example DID with namespuced idstring follows:

did:dad:Xq5YqaL6L4BpEOu7IVALOJRAU2_RxFEOALA3wYn148=blue
A DID may have optional parts including a. path, query, or fragment. These use the same syntax as a URL, that
i, the path is delimited with slashes, /, the query with a question mark, %, and the fragment with a pound sign,
#. When the path part is provided then the query applies to the resource referenced by the path and the
fragument refers to an element in the document referenced by the path. An example follows
did:dad:X5Y qaL6LASpRRTIULLOJRAU2_RxFPOALA3wYn148—/mom?who—me /}luc
In contrast, when the path part is missing but either the query or fragment part is provided then the query
and/or fragment parts have special meaning. A query without a path means tho the query is an operation on
cither the DID itsclf or the DID document (DDO). Likewise when a fragment is provided then tho fragment is
referencing an clemet of the DDO. An example of a DID without a path but with a query follows:

did:dad:Xq5YqaL6L48p£Ou7IURLOIRAU2_RAFPOALA3wYn148="whomme
As will be described later, a query part on a DID expression without & path part will enable the generation of
DDIDs {derived DIDs).
MINIMAL DAD
A minimal DAD (decentralized antonomic data) item is a data item that contains a DID or DDID that helps
miquely identify that data item or afiliated data stream. Tn this paper JSON is used to represent serialized DAD
itoms but other formats could be used instcad. An example minimal trivial DAD s provided below. Tt is trivial
becanse there is no data payload

i

"id": "did:dad:Xq5YqaL6L4BPEO£uTIURLOJRAU2_RxFPOALA3wYn148="

)
To ensure datw integity (i that the data has not been tampered with) a signature that is verifiable as being
generated by the private key associated with the public key in the id field value is appended to the DAD item.
This signature verifies that the DAD item was created by the holder of the associated private key The DAD item
s both self-ientifing and selfcertifying because the identifier value given by the id feld is included in the signed
data and is verifisble against the private key associated with the public key obtained from the associated DID in
the id field. In the example below s a trivial DAD with an appended signature. The signature is separated from
the JSON serialization with characters that may ot appear in the JSON.

While the simple DADS given in the examples above are minimally selfidentifying and self-certifying, they do not
provide support for other self management properties such as key management. In other words, because each DID
(Decentralized Identifer) references a public signing key with its associated private key, it needs to be managed as
a ey not just as an identifier, The following sections wil introduce the core key-management properties und the
associated meta-data that a DAT) needs in order to support thos propertics.
KEY MANAGEMENT
‘The three main key management operations ae:
* Reproduction
*  Rotation
* Recovery
01/26/18 Decentralizod AutonomicData. 4

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
We call these the essential throe R's of key management.

Key Reproduction

Key reproduction is all about managing the creation of new or derived keys. Lach new DID requires a new
public/private key pair. The private keys must be kept in a secured location. One reason to create unique
public/private ke pairs for each pair-wise relationship is to minimize the risk of exposure to exploits from the
repeated use of a given key pair. Another reason to croate unique key pairs for cach inforaction between partis is
a3 a means for maintaining privacy through pseudonymity. This is discussed in more detail below. Minimizing the
mumber of private keys that, must be secnrely prescrved for a given number of public keys simplifies management
and reduces both expense and risk of exposure. To reiterate, there are two key-storage issues, one is storing public
Keys and the other is securely storing private keys. An exploit that captures a. store of public keys may mean
Toss of privacy beeause the expoiter can now correlate activity associated with those public keys. An exploit that,
eaptures a store of private keys means that, the exploiter many now be able to use those private keys to take
control of any associated resonrces. Consequently, one wants to avoid storing private keys as much as possible
Privacy and Confidentiolity

One desirable feature of a DAD is that it be privcy preserving, A simplified definition of privacy is that if two
parties are participating in an exchiange of data in s given context. then the parties should not be Tinked to other
interactions with other parties in other contexts. A simplified definition of confidentiality is that the content of the
data exchanged is not disclosed to a third party. Confidentiality is usually obtained by encrypting the data. This
paper does not specifically cover encryption but in general the mechanisms for munaging encryption keys are very
similar to those for managing signing keys.

An exchange can be private but not confidential, confidential but not private, both, or neither. A minimally
sufficent means for preserving privacy s 1o use a DID as a pseudonymons identifier of each party to the exchange,
A pseudongnm is a manufactured alias (e.g. identifir) that is under the control of its ereator and that. is used to
identify u given interaction but is not linkable to other interactions by its owner, The ability of a third party to
correlute an entity's beluvior across contexts is reduced when the entity uses a unique

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
DID for each context,
Although there are more sophisticated methods for preserving privcy such as zero-knowledge proofs, the gosl
here is to use methods that are compatible with the performance demands of streaming data.

As mentioned above, the problom with usin unique pserdonymsjerypionyms for cach exchange is that a large
‘mumber of sueh identificrs may need to be maintained. Fortunately hierachically dorived keychains provide a way
to manage these cryponyms with u reasonsble level of effort.

Hicrachical Deterministic Key Generation

A3 previously mentioned, reproduction has (o do with the generation of new keys. One way lo accomplish this is
with a deterministic procedure for generating new public/private keys pairs where the private keys may be
reproduced securely from some public information without having to be stored. A hierarchically deterministic
(HD) key-generation algorithm does this by using a master or root private key and then generating new key pairs
using a deterministic key-derivation algorithm. A derived key is expressed as a branch in u tree of parent,/child
Keye. lach public key includes the path o its location in the tree. The private ke for a given public key in the
rce can be seenrly regenerated nsing the root. private key and the key path, also called a chain code. Only one
04/26/18 Decentralisod AutonomicData 5

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
private key, the root, necds to be stored.
The BIP-32 specification, for example, uses an indexed path representation for its HD chain code, such as,
“0/1/2/07. The BIP-32 algorithm needs a master or root key pair and a chain code for each derived key. Then
only the master key pair needs o be saved and only the master private key needs to be kept securely secret. The
other private keys can be reproduced on the fiy given the key generation algorithm and the chain code. An
extended public key wonild include the chain corde in its representation so that the associated private key can be
derived by the holder of the master private key any time tho cxtended public key is presented. This is the
procedure for hardened keys.
The query part of the DID syntux may be used to represent an 1D chain code or HD key path for an HD key
that is derived from a root DID. This provides an economical way to specify derived DIDs (DDIDs) that are used
to identify DADS. An example follows:

did:dad:Xq5YqaL6L48pEOfuTIURLORAU2_RAFROALA3wYn148="chain=0\1\2
This expression above discloses the root public DID as well as the key derivation path or chain via the query par.
For the sake of brevity this will be call an extended DID. The actual derived DDID is create by applying the HD
algorithm such as:

did:dad:Qt27£ThHONZSa88V Tkep6H- 4HABEES4SHONL VW 6FE=
Thus a database of DDIDs could be indexed by DDID expressions with cach value being the extended DID.
Looking up the extendod DID allows the holder to reereate on the fiy the associated privaic key for the DDID
withont; ever having to store the private key. This might look like the following:

The namespacing of the DID idstring also provides information that could be nsed to help formulate an HD path
to generate a DDID. The following example shows two different DDIDs using the same public key and the same

could be used for each unique DAD or & urique DDID could be used for each unique destination party that is

Change Detection
Stale DAD items must ofien be detectable to provent replay attacks. A later re-transmission of an old copy of the
DAD itom must not: supereode a newer copy. Using a sequence number or some other identifier could provide
change detection. Another way to provide chauge detection is for the DAD item 1o include & changed field whose
value is monotonically increasing and changes every time the data is changed. The souce of the data can enforce
that the changed field value is monotonically increasing. Typical approaches include a monotonically increasing
date-time stamp or sequence mumber. Any older data. items resent. or replayed would have older date-time stamps

Change detection prevents replay attacks in the following mauner. A second party reccives DAD updates that are
each signed by the associated private key. Bach update has a monitonically increasing changed field. The source
signer controls the contents of the data wrapped by the signature, Therefore the signer controls any changed feld,
A consistent signer will use & monotonically increasing changed value whenever the data wrapped by the signature
is changed. Thus a malicious third party cannot replay earlier instances of the DAD wrapped by a valid signature
to the orginal second party because the second party knows to discard any receptions that have older changed
felds than the latest one they have already received.
On the Fiy DDIDS in DADs
One important use case for DDIDs in DADs is to identify data that is received from a source that is not providing
identifying information with the data. The receiver then creates an associated DID and DDIDs fo identify the
data. At some later point the receiver may be able to link this data with some other identifying information or the
soureo may “claim” this data by supplying identifying information. Tn this case the DDIDs are privaie to the
receiver but. can later bo used to credibly provenance the internal s of the data. This may be extremely
beneficial when shared amongst the entitios in the processing chain as a way to manage the entailed proliferation
of keys that may all bo claimed later as a hicrarchial group. The DIDs and associated derivation operations for
DDIDS may be shared amongst & group of more-or-less trusted entities that are involved in the processing chain.
04/26/18 Decentralisod AutonomicData 8

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
Public Derivation

Another important used case for DDIDS in DADS is to avoid storing even the DDID with its derivation chain
This may be an issue when a client. wishes o conmunicate with & potenially very large number of public services.
Each public service would be & new pairing with a unique DDID. If the derivation algorithm for an HD-Key
DDID could use the public key o public DID of the public service to generate the DDID then the client need not
store the actual DDID but can secover the DDID by using the public DID of the server to re-derive the associated
DDID. ‘This can be done by creating & hush of the root DID private key and the remote server public DID to
create the seed nsed to generate the DDID for the DAD. This also means that the DDIDs or chain codes do ot
have to be included in the keys preserved by a key-recovery systern.

Key Rotation

“The simplest approach 1o key rotation is 0 revoke and replace the ey in one operation, Iu some cases revocation
without replacenent is warranted. But this is the same es revoking and then replacing with & null key. Key
rotation without revocation usually poses a security risk 50 it is not needed. Hence we simplify key management
1o include revocation as a subset. of rotation.

Koy rotation is necessary becanse keys used for sigaing (andor encryption) may suffer increased risk of becoming
compromised die to contined use over time, may be vulnerable to brite force attack merly due to advances in
computing technology over time, or may become compromised due to misuse or a specific exploit. Periodically
rotating the key bounds the risk of compromise resulting from exposure over time. The more difficult problem to
solve is secure roation aficr a specific exploit may have already oceurrod. Tn this cas, the roceiving party may
seciexe a valid signed rotation operation from the exploiter prior to the orignal halding entity sonding a valid
xotation operution. The receiver may erroneously accept o rolation operation that transfers control of the data to
the exploiler. A subsequent rotation operstion from the original holder would ither create a conflit or & race
condition for the receiver.

Althongh there are several ways to solve the early Totation exploit problem described above, the goal is to find the
‘minimally sufficiont moans for preventing that exploit that is compatible with the domands of streaming data
applications for which DADs are well suited.

Basic Prerotation

A complication with DADs is that thero a

re two types of keys being nsed: th koys for the root DIDs and the keys
for the derived DIDS (DDIDS). Generating a derived key paie requires wsing the privae root key. The process for
pre-rotating the raot, DID is described fist, followed by tho additional measures for DDID pr-rofation.

“The upproach presented hexe s to pre-rotate the DID key and declare the pre-rofation at the inception of the
DID. This pre-rotation is declared at initalization. This may be done with &n inception event. A later rotation
Operation event creates the next pre-rotated key thus propogating a new set of current key und pre-rotated key.

signature scheme whero any M-of-N signatures are required (o incept or rotate where M < N, and M, N are
integers. The total number of keys in the list is a multiple of N. The following examples provide an inception and

Coltetive Signatures
This multi-signature scheme sulfers from the siguificant inerease in the length of the attached signature block. One
way to ameliorate this “bloat” is to use collective multi-signatures, A colleetive signature has the property that its
Tength is not a multiple of the number of signatures it holds. Typically the maximum length of a collective
signature is about double the length of a non-collective signature and does not increase significantly as more
signatures are added to the collective, There is a draft IETF standard for collective signatures CoSi that might be
useful for multi-signature rotation., Some useful references are here project, peper, slides. Collective signatures are
a type of Schnorr multi-signature or Schnorr threshold signature
DDID Precotation
The complication for DDIDs (Derived DIDs) is that cach DAD stream for cach pairing of sender and recciver may
have a unique DDID. Rotation of the oot DID also requires rofating the DDIDs. The same pre-rotation
approach, however, can be used for the DDIDs. At the inception event the root key and pre-rofation root keys are
ercated. These keys are then used o ereated a sot of DDIDS and pre-rotated derived keys using the root and pre-
rotated root. keys respectively. This does not. significantly change the exploit vulnerability as the incoption event is
still one ovent. Althongh the pro-rotated root DID key is used fo eroate a set of pre-rotated derived keys, it doos
not signicantly inerease its exposure. Each rotation event then involves rotating the root DID key and all the
DDID keys. The important. consideration s that the number of DDIDs in the sel. must be determined in advance
in order 1o create all the pre-rotated derived keys at one time. This can be managed by ereating extra DDIDs and
pre-rotated derived keys at the inception event. Only the public hall of each of the key pairs need to be stored.
In contrast, creating additional DDIDs with pre-rotated keys at a later time requires using the pro-rotated root
private key. This inereases the exposure of

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
that private key to cxploits and makes it loss secure for pro-rofation
When the set of pro-rotated DDIDs is consnmed, a rotation-operation cvent may be triggered, thereby rofating
the existing DDIDs and then allowing additional DDIDs 1o be created.

Alternatively if the pre-rotated set of DDIDs is consnmed then a nese DDID tree may be created with a nique
new pre-rotated root key. This would create a hicrachy of groups of pre-rotated DDIDs and derived keys.
Moreover, when the re-establishment and re-initialization of a DAD stream is not a high-cost or high-risk
endeavor then instead of pre-rotating the DDIDs, ouly pre-rotate the root DID and just elose down the current
DAD stream and re-establish with  new DDID created by the pre-rotated key as part of the rotation even.
Finally if the exposure of the oot DID is insignificant compared the exposure of the DDIDs then another
approach to DDID pre-rotation eold bo employed. This requires a trade-off betsvoen convenicnce and privacy. A
&ronp of receivers conld all have knowledge of the root publie DID key and its pre-rotated public DID key for
their unique DDIDs. This means that the members of the group could leak correlation information about the
group via the shared root DID. Howover each member of the group could still maintain seeurily via its uniquo
DDID. Tn this case the root private DID is used to derive both the inception DDID and the pre-rotated derived
Key of each member. The individual members could then undergo DDID key rotation bt only using the root, DID
not its pre-rotated key. In the rare event that the oot DID needs to be rotated then each of the DDID members
performs a double rotation witkin a rotation event. The first rotation rotates to the pre-rotated key generated
using the original root, DID, the second rotation is to a new set of derived and pre-rotated derived keys, each
generated using the new pre-rotated root key. The first derived ey in the pair is the new signer key, the second is
the mew prerotated signer key. A receiver must have knowledge of the root DID and pre-rotated root key in order
to verify that the second rotation is not a forgery. This approach enables the organization and managment of
DDIDs in heirarchical groups where the members of cach group kuow about their group-root DID but that group-
100t DID could be a DDID of a higher level group and 50 on. Lower level groups only know about thier group zoot
DID, but mot any sibling groups so it can't leak information about sibling or parent groups only child gronps.
Replagabitity

The constraint on prerotation s that the receiving party be able to replay the rotation events to ensure that it
did ot miss a rotation. This replay allows the receiver to verify the provenance

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
chain of rotations. The question
then is what are minimally sufficient, means for enabling this replay capability?

There are two use cases for providing this replay capability. The first case is for online one-Lo-one or pairwise
interactions and the other case is for offline one-Lo-one or equivalently one-to-many or public interactions.

In the one-to-one case, there is the sender of & DAD stream and the reciever of the stream. The initiation of the
stream wonld involve exchanging keys for pairwise communication and would also incinde the establishment of
the DDID used for the DAD items sent, The first DAD sent would include the DDID for the DAD as will as the
pre-rotated DDID. This is the inception event. The rocciver then merely noeds to maintain a rnning log of DAD
itoms that contain rotation cvents. As long as reliable communications are used botween the sender and recciver,
then the receiver can ensure that it has obscrved all rofation cvents by keeping its log and no imposter can later
send an undetectable forged inception or rotation event. If the reciever loses its history then it must re-cstablish
its communications channel and re-initialize. Alternatively the sender could maintain a copy of the inception and
rotation event. history and then provide it to the receiver upon request. The receiver would cache this history for
specdier lookup. An imposter attempting to send an carler forged incoption event would be nsuceossful becaise
only the first inception event is considered valid
