author: SmithSamuelM
content:
In the ane-to-many, public, or offine case, the rotation history i maintained by a service. While a dccontralized
distribited consensus blockehain ledger conld provide this service it is not the minimally snffciont moans of
providing this capabilit. The minimally suficient means is a redundant imutable event log of nception and
rotation events indexed by the DDID associated with the DAD for the given DAD stream. The constraint is that,
a sulicient. majority of the log hosts must be non-fauly at any point in time. This includos Byzantine fanls. Is is
also assumed that. the sender communicates with the hosts using a reliable end-to-end signed protocol. The sender
broadeasts the inception event to all the redundant hosts that provide copies of the log, These hosts are called
Replicants. Then either the Replicants respond to the sender with s confirmation that the event is written to their
o or the sender reads the log to verify. The eveut history is indexed by the DDID, Buch Replicant timestumps
aud signs each entry in each event history. Each Replicant ouly llows one and ouly one inception event per event,
history. Attempts by imposters to forge an carlier inception event would be denicd by hongst Replicants. The
sender can then verify that a suficient majority of the Replicants have captured each event and have consistent
event histories, Subsequent rotation events are redundantly appended to the DDID indexed log in the same way.
The receiver can then broadcast  query to the Replicants and verity via their responses that & suffcient majority
of the Replicants have the same DDID indexed event og. This canbles both offine and one-to-many event
streams.

“This approach is more scalable than using » distributed consensus ledger becauso the Replicants do ot need to
communicate with each other. The inter-host agreement of the members of a distributed consensus pool is nsually
the limiting factor in scalablity. Morever a given receiver could be completely responsible for providing the
immitable log service for its own data stream with the sender. Each receiver conld choose to implement o
iffeent; lovel of reliability. Loss of the event log means that the sender and receiver have to re-initialize und re-
establish the DAD stream. Alternatively the sender conld be responsible for providing a sat of Replicants and
male the ovent log available to the recciver upon reqest.

Key Recovery

Key recovery is about providing a secure way of recovering a lost private key. The

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
important consideration here is
that the recovery mechanism be compatible with stzesmming data applications us per DADs. Keys recovery tends
to be & rare occurrence so performance demands muay be less constraining, Nonetheloss, finding the minimally
sufficient means for key rocavery s still the goal. Moreover, to be secnre the private key needs to be kept sccrct,
Because cryptographic keys are long strings of numbers they are extremely hard to remember, this means that
typically private keys are stored some place besides a person’s memory and are therefore subject to being lost or
stolen.

I i i required or at leust desirable that the DAD stream not be reinitialized due 10 the los of the rotation-event
history then  key-recovery mechanisin would also need (0 provide recovery of the key-rotation history. T'o
restate, it s not enongh to just recover the original zoot DID but every rotated root DID must be recovered as
well. Given that eypically rotations happen rarely, the rotation-event history should be small in size and not pose
a storagesize problem for recovery. Thus key recovery for DADs needs to at least recover the original root key
and any rotations.

DDIDS can be regenerated from the xoot DID given the HD-derivation code. In the case where the the DDID
stream may ol be easily reestablished but must resume given the latest rotated DDID then the HD chain code
st also be preservd and recovered. If e nunber of DDIDs is very large then the storage requirements for
042618 Decentralizod AutonomicData 18

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
chain codes may also be large relative to the storage requirament for key recovery. The DID root prblic key and
DDID derivation chain codes do not expose the privae keys. However, althongh disclosing the root public key and
chain code for a DDID is not a security risk, it could be a privacy risk. A third parly could correlate data streams
from the associated DDIDs should the root. public key used by multiple DDIDS be exposed. One way to address
this is to encrypi.the chain codes with an encryption key derived from the root signing key. The chain codes can
then be stored outside of the core recovery system. The worst case exploit then is a loss of privacy should the
encryption be broken but not a loss of control of the resources owned by the private key

When the DDID for communicating with a public service is derived from the public key of a server then the client
does not need to preserve and recover the HD chain code. Instead it can regencrate the DDID using a hash of the
root. private DID and the public DID of the server. A complication oceurs when the rao, private key has been
rotated and the sorver was not. made aware of the rotation. The elient can still recover the eurrent root, DID used
by the server using a trial and error approach by going through the list of rotated root, DIDs, generating the
associated DDID or derived key, verifying if the server will aceopt it, and if not incromenting to the next rotatod
root. Eventually the elient vill discover the last rotated DDID or derived ey recoguized by the serve. As a result
the client can recover the appropriate DDID or derived key for a given service without having to preserve
auything but the history of rotated root DIDs. This approach may provide meaniugful storage savings when the
‘number of external services is large.

Crptagraphic Strengih

Injormation Thearetic Security and Perfect Security

With respoct to DAD, key recovery deals with the recovery of the private half of signing and for encryption keys
in public/privato key pairs. Given that once an adversary has the private key, seourity is complotely broken, the
eryplosystems used to backup and recover private keys nowds to he as secure as is practically possible. The highest
level of crypto-graphic secruity s called information-theorelic security. A eryptosysten that hus this level of
security canniol be broken algorithimiclly even if the adversary has nearly unlimited computing power including
quantum computing, T4 must be broken by brute

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
force if at all. Brute force means that in order Lo guaranice
success the adversary must search every combination of key or seed. A special case of information-theoretie
security is called perfect sccuricy. Perfect security means that the cipher text provides no information about the
Key. There are two well-known cryptosystems that can exhibit perfect sercurity. One is sccret sharing or splitting
(see also s). The other s a one-time pad (see also oto). Correct implementation of either for a combination of
these two upproaches is appropriate for private-key recovery.

Sufficient Cryptographic Strength fo Withatand o Drute-force Atiack

For cryptosystems with perfect security, the fundamental parameter is the mumber of bits of entropy needed to
resist amy practical brute force attack. In other words, when a large random number is wsed as a seed /key to @
eryplosystem that has perfect. security, the question o be answered is how large does the random number need to
be to withsiand a brute force attack? Tn Shannon information theory the entropy of a messago is measured in bits.
The randomness of a mumber or message can measured by the mumbor of bits of entropy in the mumber. A
cryplographic quality random wumber will have s many bits of entropy as the number of bits in the number.
Assuming conventional non-quantum computers, the convention wisdom is that, for systems with information
theoretic or perfect security, the seed fkey needs Lo have on the order of 128 bits (16 bytes) to practically
withstand any brute force attack. For other cryptosystems that do not have perfect security the size of the
04/26/18 Decentralisod AutonomicData 19

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
secd ey may need to be much larger.

Theoretically, quantum computers, using Grover's Algoritiim might be able to brute force a 2" random number
with only 2°* trials. Thus once quantum computers exists the size of N might need to increase from 128 to 236,
An Nobit long base-2 random number has 2¥ difforent possible values. Given that. with perfoot security no other
information s available to an attacker, the attacker may neod to try every possible value before finding the
correct one. Thus the number of attempts tha, the attacker would have 10 test may be as much as 2" . Given
available computing power, one can estimate if 128 is s large enought N to make brute force attack impractical.
Let's suppose that the adversary has access to supercomputers, Current supercomputers can perform on the order
of one quadrillion operations per second. Individual CPU cores can only perform about 4 billion operatons per
second but a supercomputer will employ many corcs in parallel. A quadrillion is approximately 27 =
1,125,800,006,842,624. Suppose somchow an adversary had a million (2% = 1,048 576) super computers to employ
in parallel. The adversary could then try 2* * 2 = 2 values per second (assuming that each try only took one
operation). There are about 3600 * 21 * 365 — 313,336,000 — 275550 _g0 = 92 soconds in a year, Thus
this set of a million super computers could try 2% _ 9% values per year. For a 128-bit random number this
means that the adversary would need on the order of 2% % — 2% — 589,934,592 years to find the right value.
This assumes that the value of breaking the cryplosystem is worth the expense of that nuuch computing power
Consequently, a eryptosystem with perfect security and 128 bits of cryptographic strength is practically impossible
to breals,

Recovery Methods

Fundamentally key recovery involves shifting the burden of remembering a cryptographic key made of  long
random string of mumbers to some other fask that is less onerous.

Physicat Security

One approach to recovery is to shift the burden of recovery from remembering a private key o keys to protocting
Physical copics of the keys. This s callod physical socnrity. Recovery first involves creating a hard copy of the
Key(s) sich as a printed picce of paper or a.“hard” clectronic wallet and then hiding the hard copy. The memory
task now becomes remembering where the hard copy was hidden. The sccurity of the approach is now based on
the physical security of the hidden location (under the bed, in

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
the safety deposit box, in a hole in the hackyard).
The assumption is that remembering where something is hidden is sssumed to be relatively reliable. Most
importan. is that physical security is not vulncrable to remote attacks over the intornet nor computational attacks
where the attacker can employ resources and time o break u ey, The attacker must have physical access and
may be physically st risk. A weakness of this approach is that recovery may lake time. Moreover if the person
with the knowlede of the key location is ineapacitated then recovery may be impossible unless the location of
hard copy or another hard copy s shared with someonc else, thus exposing a vulnerability. One way to address
this is to se & legal mechanism such as power of attorney, a will, or another guardian who is authorized to reveal
the hurd copy given predefined circumstances. This can be ameliorated by using tamper-resistent envelopes and
physical aceess logs to increase the risk of discovery. In any event physical recovery is useful as a backup to non-
physical security recovery methods but may be too incouvenient as the primary form of recovery for the mansgers

of streaming data. applications. Tn general physical sccurity may bo a good backup for any of the other recovery.
methods.

Mnemonics

A mnemonic is & device or technique o aid human memory. The memory task in this ease is (0 remember a 128-
bit random number s o key or seed. This is rther complicated for DAD recovery as it is not sufficient to just
secover a single private key but instead requires the recovery of the whole key rotation history. One way to
accomplish this is Lo use  125-bit randoms mumber as a seed to & system that hides and zecovers the whole
rotation bistory. This will be discussed in more detail below. One well- known muemonic is to use u phrase of
random words from a word list. The user can create a story or imaginery visualation of a situation in which the
words are all represented. An example would be the words, blue cat house cat pudding. Visualizing and rehcarsing
a fantastic situation that includes objects and actions corresponding to the words makes is much casier to
remember.

"The DiceWazc(see also 1k und o) approacls consists of a word lish of 7776 words that are selected ut random
(using dice). The user must. remenuber the words and their order Lo form a phrase that can be used 10 generate
random nurnber, The EFF has produced modified versions of the word list (EFT word Jist) that have beneficial
propertics. Given a total of 7776 words, then each randomly selected word i one of 7776 choices, which provides
log,(7776) = 12.9 bits of entropy per word. To get & 125 bits of entroy the phrase would need to include ten
words. This is pretty long for a mnemonic bt not impractical as long as the user i willing to do some rehearsal
More problematic i recovering not just one key but multiple keys from a key rotation history.

Secret shoring

Another approach is to shit the task of recavery to other partics. This oan be done securely using a secrel sharing
or “splitting” approach. The secrct information s split into what are sometimes called shards. Each shard is then
shared with another party called  shard holder. Luter the shards are collected and combined to reproduce the
secret. The shard holders must either keep the shard secret or if they are going Lo store it online they need to
enciypt the shard and must then remember their encryption key. As mentioned above, secret shaing may have
perfect security. This means that storing encrypted copies of the shards online may sill be perfectly seeure as long
as an adversay cannot correlate

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
the shards as belonging to the same secret information. If correlation does oceur
then the security is mited to the type of encryption and might be more vulnerable to exploits

In order to recover the secret information tho nser must interact with the shard holders to got them to provide
their shard; that i, the recovery i imuli-party interactive. The user then conibines the shards o reconstitute the
sharad secrct. This interaction may take time and may not be reliable. A useful variation on this approac is
called threshold o Shanie sharing where only a subset of al the shards is needed 1o reconsiitute the secre. For
cxample an M of N threshold secret sharing (M < N) algorithm would share shards with N partics. Any
combination of a subset of M parties can reconstitute the secret, This allows some of the parties to ot be
available or to lose their shard aud stll have sucessful recovery. Typically, to maintain secreey the N paties do
not know of esch other.

Although the security properties of Secret, sharing make it an atiractive approach for key recovery, seerct, sharing
can e complicated, especially bocause it requircs interaetion with multiple parties. The scerot owner must recall

author: SmithSamuelM

who the N parties are o af least M non-fanlty partics. Tn an organizational setting, however, there may be a
designated group of individuals who know about and hold the shards and have a policy for circumstances under
which they can share the shards.

one-time Pad

As mentioned previously, the oxc-Lin O] (see also oto) may exhibit perfect security. The OTP is o
venerable cyphersystem that has the advantage that it can be used manually without a computer. Besically & long
string of random characters forms the pad. Somoone can use the pad w0 encrypt a plain-tex! message. The
procedure is to combine each plain-test eharacter in order with the corresponding character from the pad. The
combination is typically performed using modulo addition of the two characters but can be performed with a
bitwise XOR. Because characters from the pad may only be used once, the pad must be at leust a6 long as the
plain-text message, The one time use of » randorn string of characters from the pad is what gives the system its
perfect security property. If two parties wish to exchange multiple messages, then the pad nust be at least as long
as the sum of the length of all the messages. The main disadvantage of a one-time pad is that the two parties
‘must each obtain & copy of the same pad. This is less of a disadvantage for key recovery because the the
encrypted message (keys) does not need to be exchanged with another for decryption but are decrypted by the
self-same party so only one copy of the pad is needed.

Suppose for example, a OTP is used to encrypt the key or key history. Given that the adversary docs not have
access to the OTP then the eneryption has perfect seereey which means that. the only viable attack is via brute
foree. 17 the enerypied key or key history s at Ioast 128 bits long then brute forco is practicaly impossible
Consequently the OTP encrypted key history could be safely stored in a public immutable database. The
remaining problem is management of the OTP. Using an OTP 1o encrypt the ke history just creates a new
problenn, that, of securing the OTP itself. But the main advantage of a OTP over secret, sharing deseribed above
for ey recovery is that a OTP approach is non-multi-party interactive. It can be self-contained which is
advantageous in data streaming applications.

One common but weaker variant of the OTP is the book eyper. Tn this variant the OTP is a book. Becanse the
characters in a book are not a random string there is some degree of correlation

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
between characters that makes it
Tess than perfectly sccure. Thus two parties who each have a copy of the same book (same edition) can use the
eharacters in the book as the OTP to encrypt. messages without ever having to exchange copies of the book.
Essentially using  book as OTP s an cxample of hiding the OTP in plain sight. An adversary would have to
guess that a book was being used as a one-time pad and then figure ont which book. For key rocovery, the key
owner merely needs to remember which book and edition. Should the book used by the key owner be lost, the key
owner can get another copy from a bookstore.

The book cypher is an interesting example due to the combination of simplicity, the use of existing but readily
available sonrces of information, and the ability to hide the OTP as book in plain sight. This has the advantage
that the only the titlo and edition of a book need to be remembered thus making light demand on human
‘memory. The primary disadvantage of the book cypher is that the text is not random and its difficult to caleulate
how many bits of entropy are lost for a given book.

Hybrid Key Recovery Method

One of the main atiractions of using a one-time pad (OTP) for key recovery, in contras to socrot sharing, is that

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
it is non-multi-party interactive. A hybrid approach that makes a beneficial trade-off is to nse 2 mnemonic merely
to generate a scod for a eryplographic strength psucdorandom number generator (CSPRNG). The scod is then
used via the CSPRNG to generate a OTP that is then used o encrypt the key-rotation history. The
cryplographic strength of the OTP is now governed by the length of the sed not the length of the pad. But key-
rotation histories arc relatively short compared to the period of CSPRNG so a strong enough seod (128 bits of
entrophy) would still be sufficient. for this task.

The PRNG algorithm must be of cryptogeaphic quality otherwise it conld bocome a sonrce of vuilncrability. A
recent; advancement;in CSPRNG algorithms is a chaotic itcration psuedorandom mmber generators (CIPRNG:
These are of cryptographic quality have extremely high statistical randomness. They pass both the NIST and
DicHard tests for PRNG with periods on the order of 10° apt. The basic concept. is a chatic finite state machine
clin. Unfortunately there do not yel appear (o be any open source implementations of (Lis algorithra. A more
practial CSPRNG that, could be used 10 generate a OTP from a seed is the libsodium
zandombytes_buf_deterministic function. This uses ChaCha20 under the hood.

The advantage of this hybrid approach is that the key recovery memory task is now limited to merely recovering
the seed that wonld then be used to reproduce the OTP that would then be used in turn to decrypt the key
history. This approach does not xequire multi-party interaction like scorct sharing as the seed is dircetly recovered
by the owmer via a mnemonic device, not from others. This hybrid approach still benefits from the propertics of
the OTP for encryption so that the key-rotation history can be cnerypied and stored online for recovery.

What remains then is the selection of a muemonic for generating the seed. It may be difficult for a single
‘memonic to provide & random source of seed material ot the required strength of 128 bits. Concatenating severel
sources of mnemonically derived sced material, however, conld produce the roquired strength. This is akin to the
DiceWare approach to passphrase generation. One problem with concatenation of seed material is that the order
of concatenation must also be remembered. One way to avoid having to remember the order when combining
multiple sources of seed material is to use the simple version of sccret splitting, In this form of secret splitt

ing, the
secrei.is divided into shards and each shard is XORed togother to recover the soeret. Tn this case the secret is the
sed and each shard contributes a cortain amount of entropy to the final sced. This allows & mnemonic for cach
shard that may have much less than the required 128 bils of entropy but the combination of shards could have
the required entropy and the order of the shards is not important. A non-ordored combination loscs some
cryplographic strength because the number of possibilties is no longer merely the multiple of the independant
possibilities from each shard (permutations) but is instead the number of combinations of the shards,

Suppose that there are four shards that each contribute 35 bits of entropy o in other words each shard is

sufficient. A lower bound on the nnmber of combinations of N things taken K af a time is (N/K)* (see bonnds)
The bits of cryptographic strength of the combination of four shards cach with 35 bits is where N = 2° and K —
4. Using the approximation gives the mumber of possiblities to be at least (27/2%)! = 2%=2'% This corresponds

Tho ane romaining challenge then is to find good mnemonically recoverablo sources of random seed material. One
feature that makes the The book cypher was attractive bocans it took advantago of information that was highly
available but hidden in plain sight and whose source was easy Lo remember (a book title). The problem with
books is that the content is not highly random so it in itsell is not a good source of seed material. In other words,
the challenge is to ind sources of information for secd material that have much higher degree entropy than a book
but are still aasy to romermbor. More specifcally this means finding sources of highly random soed material that
ave highly available (thus do not requive sdditional infrastructure to buckup) but are aso essentially bidden in
plain sight and easy to recall via a mnemonic device. What follows are several viable sources of mnemonically
recoverable sources of random seed material.

Diceare eed Recovery

“The DiceWare approach can be repurposed to provide a mmemonic source of soed material, These can be used to
recover the seed for the onetime pad used to encrypt the key-rotation history. Ten randomly seected words from
 DiceWare-compatiblo wordlist conld b used to genorate the seel for the one-timo pad. Ten randamly sclected
words in ondor provide tho roquired 128 bits of entropy (recall that each DiccWare word provides 12. bits of
cntropy). The order of the words is important. Fach word wonld be hashed using SHA-2 or Blake to generate a
16-byte string. Tho seed is created by concatenating tho hashes in the defined order. Once tho seed for the OTP is
generated, the rest of the recovery method follows the process described above for generating the OTP using a
CSPRNG and then using that to enerypt,/decrypt the key rotation history. The mnemonic load for this method is
the recall the order of ten words from the DiceWare or EFF wordlist. This has a large mnemonic load 5o it would
require some rehearsal and might not be very practical. Tn addition to the mnemonc at least a physical backup of
the ten words should also be created. The physical backup of the ten words could be split into parts to make it
more secure. If practical, a multi-party threshold secret sharing backup could also be created.

Github Secd Recovery

Github.com stores versioned code repositories. The associated git utility automatically caleulates a 160 it (20
byte) SHA-1 hash of each commit to a repository. These hashes are easily readable from t

The GitHub.com based recovery mechanism can be summarized as follows: Randomly choose four GitHub.com
Tepository commits. For each commit, the pairing of a project name, repository name and commit date must be
remembered and for backed up using a hardware backnp. Generate a seed by XORing together the 20-byte
commit SHA-1 commit hash from each of the four repositories. Use this seed with a deterministic CSPRNG to
gonerato a one-time pad of length at loast as long as the key rofation history. Enerypt. the key rotation history by
bitwise XORing cach byte in the history with the corresponding byte from the one-time pad. Securcly discard the
oue-time pad. Store the encrypted key-rotation istory in a bighly available database. This encrypted istory
should be inpervious to attack 5o it can be stored online. When recovery is required, remember the four

project frepositorycommil-date pairings or restore from a hardware backup. Use the pairings to lookup the SHA
1 commit hashes from GitHub.com for each. Then recreate the sced by XORing the four commit hashes. Use the
seed and the same CRPRNG to regenerate the one-time pad. Retrieve the key history from the database, Use the
one-time par to bitwise XOR each byte of the saved encrypted key bistory to unencrypt it. The key bistory is
now recovered.

The memory load is fonr triples of a project name, a repository name, and a date, or twelve itoms total, but the
order of the triples is not, important. Given that typically cach GitHiub project has  small number of repositorics,
‘merely remembering the projoct should make remembering the repository much easier by going to the projoct.
page and looking at the choices for repositorics. The date is the hardest memory task. There are soveral well
Known mnemonic techniques for remembering dates. Tn addition 1o the mnemonic, a physical backup of the hashes
should also be creatod. The physical hackup could e split into four parts to make it more seeure. IF practical &
threshold multi-party scerete sharing system cold provide additional backup.

FlckR.com Seed Recovery

The FlickR.com-based recovery mechanismn is similar to the Github.com bused one, There are over 10 billion
primary photos on FlickR. Each primary photo may come in multiple resolutions. A given photo is displyed on
the FlickR.com web page using a low-resolution copy. This displayed version can be scraped from the page, The
Flickr.com website does not provide hashes of the images, so one would have to scrape or do

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
wnload an image and
then caleulate the hash after the fact. A viable approach would be to use SHA-2 from the OpenSSL library or
Blake from the libsodium library. Ten billion is about 2% which corresponds to about 33 bits of entropy when
randomly selected. Four randomly selected images are needed to get the required 128 bits of entropy, that s, 4 *
33 = 132, If we combine the hashes from four images by XORing (ic. simplo sccrot splitting) then the number of
choices becomes the combinations of 10 billion things taken fonr af a time. As doscribed above, the lower bonnd
on the number of combinations of ¥ things taken K at a time is (N/K)™. This gives the mumber of possibilities to
be (2%/2%)* — 2"'_9L This corresponds to 124 bits of entropy which s close enough to the required 125, (2 —
16, which is not meaningfully weaker as it wonld still take 500,000,000 years to break). The procedure for recovery
is essentially the same as the GitHub example above, once the hashes for each photo have been generated.

The muemonic task is remernbering four images. Humans are very good at rementbering images given o selection,
The hard nnenonic task is searching on FIickR for & given image using tags. It takes sbout four or five tags to
get the lst of images to under 100 for a given tag set. The mnemonic task is then to remember four sels of four to
five tags each, where the Lags are not in any order. Remembering which photo is helped by the fact tha the tag
set typically corresponds to features of the photo. Moreover, imuges provide an opportunity to hide them in plain
sight. In sddition Lo the mnemonic, a physical backup of the hashes should also be created. The physical backup
could be spli into four parts to make it more secure. If practical a threshiold multi-party secret shuriag system

‘Market and Technology Opportunity White Paper 2
arger entity may have its own dedicated data-centers. A public cloud, on the other hand, is composed of servers in data-centers
owned und minsged by an entity that rents the use of those servers 10 ofher entities, Typically the servers in a public cloud all
belong to the same network, but each server may have its own firewall that limits access t0 tself. The public cloud host enity
provides IT stafl to manage and maintain the hardware as part of the rentl fec. In order 10 be able to manage its rented servers
the public cloud host IT staff has login access to the operating systems of those serves. There are also Aybrid clouds that have
different compositions of private and public components and conirol over those components[36; 551,

Traditionally, public clouds were used in concert with privatc clouds to add elastcity 10 the private cloud in order 1o scale
capacity up and down in response to dynamic variations in load. This reduced the need for expensive overcapacity to handle
dynamic peak processing loads. Thus, despite being more expensive per unit of computation, public clouds, on balance, are oflen
‘more cconomical overal by avoiding the expense of unuscd capacity. Increasingly morcover, because of the convenience of not
having to maintain dedicated computation infrastructure including the associated IT staff, especially for smaller enttis, public
clouds are not just being used 10 augment private clouds but are instead completely replacing private clouds. Furthermore the
operational advantages of expensing the rental of infrastructure as a service (IaaS) versus maintaining infrastructure as a capital
asset s attractive {0 startups, Onee an entity replaces ts private cloud infrastructure with public ts diflcult (0 ever go back. One
factor stopping a wholesale migration to public cloud from private cloud are concerns about security and privacy of the public
cloud. In one report 66.5% of companies with private cloud are concerned about public cloud securlty threats o sensitive data
and 56.9 percent wiere concerned about third party account compromise] 17]. A elated problem is the competitve threat posed by
public cloud providers, such as, Microsoft,that also market software applications. Soon after Microsoft acquired the cloud based
source code repository service, Github, over 100,000 plus users switched o competing services citing, as one of man, concerns
over the confideniality of their source code in tha

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
t hands of a potential competitor [36; 72],

Historically, in terms of market share for application workloads, private clouds had a much bigger share relative o public
clouds. This has been rapidly declining, one estimate is that in 2016 the ratio of private to public cloud application workloads
was at 60.9/39.1%. Whereas by 2018 this raio will have flipped, such that the majority 53.8% of application workloads will be
in public clouds [17; 241,

‘What makes the frend of increasing public cloud dominance so concerning is that a handful of Fortune 100 providers control
most of public cloud infrastructure and that portion is increasing relative to their competition(73]. In terms of revenue, in 2016
Amazon had a 40 percent share, with Microsoft, Google, and TBM together making up a 23 percent share for a tota of 63%. That
total share was an increase of 5 percent from 2015 [67]. It is predicted that in 2018 this dominance will grow such that the
combined Amazon, Microsoft, and Gioogle share of the public cloud market will be 76 percent and keep growing t© 80 percent
by 2020 [74]. Thus while the public cloud is captaring more and more of the overall compute infrastructure market share, the top
four public cloud providers, with Amazon much more than the other three combined, e capfuring an increasingly larger relative
share of the public cloud market. This trend towards a technical monopoly of compute infrastructure s the data cconomy
becomes an increasingly valuable segment of all economic activity is particularly worrisome

From the broader perspective of answering the question of who will capture the most value from the data cconomy, the five
largest companies by market cap are Apple, Amazon, Microsof, Alphabet({Google), and Facebook[118]. In May of of 2018 the
combined market cap of these five was over 3.7 trillion USD[136] and is increasing. Apple and Amazon for the frst ime cach
surpassed the one frillion mark[118]. Notable is that three of the five are also the three largest public cloud providers and all
derive a significant share of their value from the monetization of their large private clouds.

‘The most important insight, however, to understand in terms of recognizing opportunitics is not the landscape of currnt
computing infrastructure as controlled by a handul of cloud providers, but to understand what the landscape of future computing
infrastructurc could become. As mentioncd above, the data cconomy is increasingly driven by data coming from into

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
met
comnected devices. One estimate gives the number of intenet conmected devices at 11 billion in 2016, tripling to 30 billion by

2.1. Spare Capacity

The base commodities needed for a public fog computing infrastructure are space, eneray, and bandwidth t0 house, poswer, and

data-centers [69), although viable, that approach still requires purchasing space, power, and bandwidth for those micro-data-
centers. Our approach is to use spare space, energy, and bandwidth that s already available i residential, and commercial
buildings. Of these the limiting constraint is bandwidth. Fortunately there i an increasing availability of unused bandwidth
especially in residences with broadband connections. Bandwidth is often measured in GB (gigabytes) of data transfer. Total
available data transfer can be estimated by multiplying the intemet connection speed in Gibps by a time interval in seconds, such
as, seconds per month. Average spare bandwidth in GB is then just the average available bandwidth minus the average used
bandwidth. Estimates of average and median intemet connection speed vary widely and often combine mobile and fixed

2.3. Ephemeral Computing

2.4. Federated Computing

Federated computing is where cooperating distributed servers together perform computing tasks [36). Federated computing
coordinates computing resources from disparate locations to provide a shared computational resource. Historicaly, federation
‘has only been used on large computing tasks such as high performance, grid, or big data computing. The approach of building
mare complex services from a set of serverless functions i a new type of federated computing. Typically federated compuing
has only been implemented with a centralized coordinator. Federating decentralized fog computing resources can be problematic
due to the diffuse control of the associated compuling resources and the extremely sparse nature of any single providers's fog
resources. This may require federating fog resources among disparate providers; That is the challenge. Localty concentrated fog
computing resources, however, may provide the best price for performance for edge computing; That is the opportunity.
Fortunately, with an appropriate decentralized architecture, federation techniques can be applied to combine a group of small
disposable servers running a serverless framework to perform a bigger computing task, that is, a full fledged cloud-like service,
butin the fog.

2.5.1. Distributed Consensus

‘The most promising method for decentralized coordination of resources under the control of multiple parties s a distributed.
consensus algorithm. Distributed consensus is the process by which a pool of computing nodes come o agrcement about a
sequence of events. This sequence might b transactions in a ledger, or entrics in a database, or the order of operations in a state
‘machine, There are three main classes of distrbuted consensus algorithms,these are: Proof of Work (POW) [98; 112; 131), Proof
of Stake (POS) [97; 145), and Byzantine Agreement (BA) [7; 15; 20; 22; 32; 33; 40]. There arc many variants of the three main
classes of BFT algorithms. What they all share is a featue called Byzantine Fault Toerance (BFT) [15]. Simply, BFT means that
that despite faulty behavior that includcs malicious acts by  minority of the members, the pool as & whole vl stll come to

By way of comparison, the basic concept of PoW is thal computing nodes competc 1o Solve a cryplographic puzzle. The
winner is the first one to broadcast proof of the solution. The winner becomes the author of the next block of fransactions,
Variants use different types of cryptographic puzzles or work. Some variants use actusl or uscful computation work. Oftn the
type of work is best suitcd for differen types of computing hardware such as ASIC, FPGA, or GPU. PaW is a simple algorithm
but s drawbacks are high latency and low throughput

In contrast, the basic concept of Pos i that some verifiable saking function biases the selcction of the node that s the author
of the next biock of transactions. One way 1o think of PoS is as an asymmetric proof of work where a larger stake reduces the
amount of work. Thosc with larger sakes win fuster and more ofien. Thus PoS has lower latency and higher throughput than
PoW, albcit with more complexity to manage the staking. A popular variant of Pos is Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS) where
participants vole their take (o elect the author(s) of the next block of ransactions [35]. Another el known varianl is Federaled
Proof of Stake (FPoS) where participants scloct a subsct of nodes o come to consensus on their specific transactions [32].
Further variants are sharded or hierarchical PoS where the nodes are broken up into sub-groups (shards) that form consensus on &
subsct of transactions

content:
and then merge the subscts 142,

Predating both PoW and PoS are the BA algorithms. Tn BA algorithms the number nodes in the poo is fixed. The first popular
usable BA algorithm was called Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT) [20]. In PBFT, the nodes in the pool clect a leader,
The leader then authors the next block of transactions and submits it to the pool for validation using a threc phase commit. The
main advantage of BA i that compared to both PoW and PoS, the latency is much lower and the throughput is very much higher.
The disadvantage of BA is complexity and the number of pool members much be kept low. Several variants of PBFT have been
developed. Notable is Redundant Byzantine Fault Tolerance (RBFT) which is more resilient [7). Others include Fast Byzantine
Consensus (FBC) [77]. Others use some type of dirccted acyclic graph (DAG) to that makes the consensus leaderless and more
asynchronous [71; 114]. Finally a hybrid variant of BA and PoS, which we call Randomized Byzanine Agreement, uses a
verifiable random function to come to conscnsus [43; 99]. Varianis include biasing the random function by the stake Lo sclcet the
author(s) of the next block or ofher similar ideas [8; 50; 103; 135). In the last two years there has becn an cxplosion in the
number and type of variants of BFT distrbuted conscnsus algorithms [26; 149] Tt s beyond the scope of this paper to mention
all of them or to describe any of these in detail

‘The design of a BFT implementation requires making tradeofls between the features of the different algorithon classes. The
sulient features that usually onc must rade-oif are sccurity, scalability, and governance. There arc four primary design clements
that a BFT distributed consensus implementation must specify and provide, these are:

+ The governance policy for selecting pool members

+ The distributed consensus algorithm(s)

+ The data stracture holding the events managed by the pool.

+ The reward mechanism for incentivizing participation of the pool members.

‘The resultant distributcd consensus system s then used 1o provide transactions that support a given application or applications. A
systm design task is to composc the best combination of these four clements to best mach the transactions for the application at
hand. For example onc very common data structur used in distributed consensus systems is a ledger. Because the pool members

to cnable integrity verification. It is notable that the term blockchain is now commonly used to mean more than just a fype of
data structure [129]. s more general meaning refers to & host of technologies thal include eryplo-currencies, distributed ledgers,
distributed consensus, modern crypto, and decentralized identity to name a few. Solving a business problem using blockchain in
iis more gencral sense means selccting and integrating @ speilic mix of the aforementioned technologies. The particular mix is
typically targeted to the application that solves the busincss problem.

“The most popular BFT distributed consensus implementations arc PoW distributed ledgers. These includes BitCoin and
‘Ethercum [13; 41]. The altractive feature of PoW i that the pool govemance policy is included as part of the PoW conscnsus
determination. This allows for open public participation of an indeterminate mumber of pool members. As prviously mentioned,
the main limitation of PoW is high latency and low throughput. PoW by itself is not scalable 1o thousands of transactions per
sccond. Consequently much of the recent innovation in bitcoin techmology has been 1o find ways to address the latency and
throughput limitations of PoW. One solution is o use a PoS system, which usually has higher throughput and lower latency than
PoW but may requirc more carcful manag

ement and governance in determining the stake. As previously mentioned, PoS can be
thought of as an asymmetric PoW where the size of the stake determincs how much work must be done. Tn contrast, BA

2.5.2. Hybrid Systems

The approach that we believe has the most promise is to use a hybrid system. In a hybrid system, muliple distributed
consensus mechanisms and pools given & more optimal combination of securiy, scalability, and goverance. For example, one
way (o achicve both high sccurity and high throughput is 1o usc a PoW ledger as a periodic anchor to transactions that happen on
another faster non-PoW auiliary distributed consensus pool. This is often called a side-chain [2]. Another approach s to use a
different consensus mechanism for pool governance than for transuetion ordering. In the fog computing application, computc job
allocation must be completed with low latency and at high throughput, Cansequently, the only viable choice for the auxiliary
consensus algorithm s a type of BA algorithm. Because pool membership goverance is not built into BA algorithms, using BA
requires implemening a governance policy o algorithm to manage pool membership. Because pool membership changes are
relatively infrequent event, however, a slower consensus algorithim such as PoW/PoS can be used to automate pool membership
govemance. Periodically anchoring the BA pool cither to a much lirger or to multiple PoW/PoS pools provides increased
security while sill enabling very high tansaction rates, We originated a form of this called ChainViail [108]. A distrbution
algorithum such us network distance or somme type of distributed hash table (DHT) to assien transactions to dillerent pools makes
it scalable. We believe thatin general this s the most viable approach for decentralized fog computing infrastructure.

We have deep experience with BA algorithms. One is Plenum, a modern BA implementation based on the RBFT algorithm
hosted by the Hyperl.cdger Indy project (95]. We have also originated a next generation BA algorithm called TOBA (Timeliness
Ordered Byzantine Agreement). TOBA s a leaderless asynchronous hybrid algorithm that combines elements of a conventional
‘multi-phase commit with a directed acyclic graph (DAG) to make it both more resilient and more scalable. One advantage of
TOBA is

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
that the core algorithm lends itself to an automated reasoning algorithm that ageregates proof of useful work, delegated
proof of stake, and expected reliabilty to sefect pool members (governance).

2.3, Design Issues

A careful examination of the scalability proposals for PoW blockehains such as Ethereum and Bitcoin reveal that scalability is
not possible with a single distributed ledger that tracks all transactions as a single asset class. Proposed fixes include some
combination of sharding (multiple asset classes with receipts between shards) or side chains [2; 142]. A more scalable approach
i to use triple signed receipts on transactions amongst mutually disjoint sets of parties to the transaction [S3; 54; 84; 101 125
136; 143]. The receipts provide non-repudiable proof of the agreement at each stage of a ransaction. The consensus pool acts a
trusted third party to sign the receipts that clear transactions within a given asset class or between two or more asset classes.
These completed transactions can then anchored to @ slower cryptocurrency based blockchain such as BitCoin and thereum.

A decentralized identity system provides the core mechanism for allowing interaction between different pools and different
asset classes. A performant asynchronous peer-to-peer communications protocol that uses decentralized identity makes the
approach scalable. Both TOBA and the first implementation of Plenum use the RAET (Reliable Asynchronous Event Transport)
protocol [107]. It s based on NaCL (LibSodiam) and CurveCP which are recognized as the state of the art open source ECC

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
‘Market and Technology Opportunity White Paper 7
crypto libraries for key exchange, signing and encryption. RAET is an inherently scalable UDP/LP based protocol that uses best
prctices for sccure relisble communications. We originally developed the open source RAET protocol for highly distributed
cloud infrastructure automation, so it is well suited for fog infrastructure automation. One of the primary concerns of  fog
computing infastructure is the management and control of the computation and data on remote edge devices. Management of
these devices requircs a sealable reliable sccure communications protocol. RAET is that protocol.

Building disuributed applications requires interoperable. communication protocols 1o conneet. software componets on
disparate servers. While there arc many communication protocols that may be employed 10 connect software components, the
most universal approach is t use 1P (Intemet Protocol) based protocols. Indeed, one of the biggest challenges in fog computing
for LoT applications is the fragmentation, stratification aad siloization of existing IoT devices due to the rampant use of non-IP
based protocols. Historically, the limited computing resources of embedded 1T devices motivated the used of resource
constrained protocols, but every year the fronter of applications that are too constrained for IP protocols s shrinking. The CoAP
projectis an exumple of lightweight LP implementations that are [rendly for consirained resource networks [25]. Thus nop-IP
networks are now generally only desirable as legacy point optimizations, not universal solutions. The only viable universal
solution for a public fog computing infrastructure s an Intemet Protocol (IP) centric approach that enables reliable, secure,
decentralized integration across and through legacy 10T silos and strata. Consequently Difuon s IP based. Aceess toffrom other
data sources and sinks that are not IP based may be provided via gateways. Moreover Difuon's IP centric nature makes it very
convenient to leverage a multtude of cloud based software tools, protocols, databases, frameworks, and platforms,

Many blockchain applications involve some form of smart contract, that i, rules that execute in  trusted but automated way
to manage a given contractual relationship. An important consideration in this contract management is that the rules be
Verifiable. We have developed a flow based programming and decision-making framework called loflo that uses a model based
approach t

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
o program synthesis and analyses that allows for easier verification [105; 106]. This was originally developed for
autonomous vehicle systems. Toflo is asynchronous and thercby provides  framework for building scalable backends for data
processing of transactions. loflo is a type of flow-based programming framework with many unique features that make it
applicable to highly distributed asynchronous reactive systems [18; 31; 44; 57 63; 64; 70; 92; 127; 128]. Difuon repurposes it to
provide a fog centric serverless computing platform. The curent "serverless” frameworks are cloud centric and largely
proprietary. Because it i a flow-based programming framework it is uniquely suitable for IOT systems. loflo itself is mature but
the repurposing as a dynamically allocated functional service is new

Important to any blockehain application is privacy preservation. Typical fully public blockchains are problematic. The path
forward is to use public information as a trust anchor but the vast majoriy of information is exchanged in pairwise transactions
that are off-chain. We are innovators i this approach. Decentralized autonomic data (DAD) items based on DIDs (Decentralized
Identifiers) enable streaming data in a scalable but secure manner [34; 110; 117]. Using portable W3C Decentralized Identifiers
(DIDs) provides the foundation for an open decentralized trust system. This enables a portzble decentralized atribute based
identity for every entity and every thing including every item of data. Decentralized Autonomic Data item (DAD) chains build
‘with DIDs provide sclf-contained end-to-end data provenance of cach data flow
2.6. Diffuse Trust Computing

Federation requires that disparate entites cooperute 1o share resources, Shring resources raises the risk that data or assets
‘may be compromised because the of the insbility of onc cniity 10 trust the actions of another. The conventional approach to IP
sccuriy is often described as defense-in-depth where cach nested layer forms a sccure perimeter that restricts access to higher
levels of trust. These perimeters typically consist of some form of host:port bused firewall. The outermost layer faces the "wild-
wild-west” of the open intemet where cvery interaction may be hostile. The next layer may be in a DMZ andor behind
fixewall, The following layer might be a trusted servies running on a private network. VPNs provide a way for an cxtormal
remotc host to tunnel through the outer layers (o access a truted space. What a per

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
imeler model assumes is that nctwork locality
can b trusted. Tnternet hosts and scrvices running in an inner layer arc allowed to sharc information with cach other because
they are in a trusted “safe” space. This conventional approach fals for many reasons. Fundamentally it fuls begause it docs not
account for the fact that hostile uscrs exist cverywhere ot just on the open intermct. Any mechanism that allows for lower
privileged users o access @ network layer provides an attack vetor for that user 10 elevale their privileges thereby gaining aceess
o everything in that layer. Henc, network locality is not sccurity. Morcover, perimeter sccurity s sttic and docs not respond to
dynamically evolving exploits.

“The solution is to use  diffcse trust perimeter-ess sccurity model. This is an extension of an earlicr approach known as a zero-
trust security model. A simplc way of cxplaining the zero-irust sccurity model is the mantra, never frust, ahways verify. The
paradigm of Zero-Trust Networking was frst popularized in 2013 by the a NIST report [47]. More recently the principles have
received much broader atication [S1; 144]. Calling it zero-frust or trust-les is a misnomer. There is still trst, 1t s just applicd in
such a way that security is enhanced. We have extended this model to accommodate computing resources under decentralized
control [109; 110; 117, We call this a diffse trust perimeter-less security model because the secutity policy is maintained with
decenralized resources that diffuse the trust in such a way that security is even more greatly enhanced than merely 7ero-{rust, A
computing infrastructure that is built from the ground up using a diffse rust perimeter-less security model, We call difese-trust
‘computing. We belicve this is the only truly viable approach for a public fog computing inffastructure. To our knowledge, Difuon
is the first decentralized public fog computing infrastructure based on diffse trust computing principles and may be the first
public computing infrastructure of any kind based on these principles

The assumptions and principles of diffse frust perimeter-less security are: (see [109; 117))

+ Policies must be governed by distributed consensus.

+ By default, cach data flow including all ransformations must be end-to-cnd provenanced using decentralized identifiers

(DIDs) and hence decentralized autonomic data (DAD) items.

The first stcp to implementing diffusc trust computing principles is the recognition that any [P network is always hostile. This
‘means all servers must be hardened using best practices for ports and services, Using end-to-end encryption and torage prevents
exploits from anyone that mercly has access to the nctwork or the data storage device. By authenticating and authorizing cvery
network interaction or data flow, security becomes granular. A successful exploit of one interaction does not bleed into any other.
Compromising one data flow docs not compromisc any other. Escalation opporlunities arc minimized. Many sceurity exploits are
discovered through repeated probes and experiments to find bugs, buffer overflows, or weaknesses in network protocols or
software implementations, Dynamic policy modification that uses & reputation Al to first profile and detcct anomalous behavior
and then restrict the authorization of that uscr prevents discovery. This adds time as a defensc. Extending the model to use
distributed consensus o goven the policy management including authentication and authorization enhances the security over
using & centralized policy manager. Distributed consensus diffuses the trust for any policy decision to a group of hosts. In order
o defeat the policy, an attacker must exploit some majority of the hosts. This makes exploits exponentially more difficult.
Morcover. distributed consensus also allows for the use of decentralized computing resources for hosting the policy munagement
pool and decentralized governance of the hosts. The final principle further exionds the decentralized trust model fo allow

The diffuse trust security model by itself addresses the security concerns of a_decentralized public fog computing
infrastructure. The other two main concens are privacy and agency. We call these the SPA (Security, Privacy, Agency) design
principles,

- Secuity

- Privacy

- Agency

Sccurity, privacy, and agency are all cxtremely important lo 4 decenalized public computing infrastructure. Many
implementations only atiempt (o address one or two of the three. Difuon s designed to provide reasonable levels of all three. As
previously mentioned distributed consensus helps improve sccurity in the open.

Privacy is about minimizing the disclosure and leakage of of information to uninvolved third parties that may exploit it
harmifully. For example, & log computing trensaction is typically between two diflerent partes, the compute provider and the
user of that compute. In order o consummatc a transiction, the partics may need to share information about cach other before
proceeding with the transaction. In addition, the transaction itself may involve the exchange of information. This is obviously the
case for fog computing transsctions. A modular approach 10 privacy allows the participants (o exchinge on-chain (public
distributed consensus pool) as litle as possible and exchange the bulk off-chain (private peer-to-peer). A detailed exposition of
privacy concens and methods s beyond the scope of this paper but the core principles may be found in the provided references
[108; 111; 122, 123], Although the citations include white-papers from the Sovrin Foundation, the Difuon Fog is not an
implementation of Sovrin but is based on similar principles such as privacy by design, minimum disclosure, and pair-wise
unique identificrs for cach relationship. Like Sovrin and others in the Decentralized Iden

‘The BA pool also prescrves privacy by using a combination of group capability atiributes and blinded routes. Group capbility
means that all members of the group share the same minimum capabilitics Thus the BA pool can make matches from the order
ook with a group of viable compute nodes that have the minimum capability for the transaction without indicating a specific
‘member of the group. The requesting node then selects off-chain the specific member(s) from the group of potential providers it
wishes 1o engage with for its compute 1asks. An attacker would have (o simultancously exploit all the members of the group
thercby making the exploit exponentially morc cxpensive. A blinded route uses blinded signaturcs to provide verifiable but not
publicly traccable network addresses to the potential participunts in a ulure computing trnsuction [L1; 30; 39; 134]. This is a
type of zero-knowledge proof [150]. This i similar to how blinded signatures arc used in E-Cash and E-Voting applications [16].
“Thus each participan, source or sink of dta, can verify that the route to cach other provided by the BA pool s vali

order book but the BA pool has no exploitzble knowledge of the routes. As a result, the data may be transferred and the
computation performed off-chain in a pair-wise private manner, Once the computation is complete, the exploitable value of the
data exchanged is minimized. In addition, using self contained DADI based blockehins enables full end-to-end traceability and
verifiability of a computation flow. Each member of a BA pool must be ablc to communicate with every other member. This is
shown symbolically in the following diagram.

To improve performance and minimize latency, multiple BA pools will be cmployed, cach in a given zone grouped by network
distance. Network distance may be measured by the round tip time, . latency for  packet o traverse the network from one
host to another and back sgain. Usually the network distunce is a function of the number of router hops it encounters along the
path. Because routers tend 1o be geopraphically distributed, using network disance also tends to result in a geographic
distribution of BA pools. This is not exact as local data-centers may have high speed low ltency hops to more remote data-
centers. Offcn the latency is a function of the cost of the comnction. Using network distance allows sources and sinks of data o
e matched to minimize average latency and or cost or some combination of the two. The number of BA pools will expand as the
network grows. The set of BA pools forms a distributed partally overlapping partal mesh of pools where the partisl overlap
between pools is with respect 10 that poof's nearest neighbors in network distance. The partial overlap provides redundant aceess
by a given participant to muliple BA pools to incroase aveilability and minimize common-mode fuilures. The partial mesh
means that some pools can anchor their iransactions with respect to some other pools but not all other pools. This is the chain-
‘mail approach to increasing security without detracting from throughput [108]. In addition, the partial mesh means that some
pools may make transactions that infersect participants between two different pools. This i a type of sharding. A hicrarchy of
ntersecting pools will allow any set of participants to mutually engage in transactions albeit at increased latency. Fortunately the
actual data transfer for compute happens pair-wisc peer-1o-peer of-chain and therefore ts ltency is not affected by the hicrarchy
of intersecting pools. T

27.2. Relay Network

Augmenting the netswork of BA pools is a relay nefwork of server nodes. One function of the relay nefwork is provide read-
only access to copies of the distributed ledgers and databases maintained by the BA pools. The relay netwark thercby reduces the
read load on the BA pool members. As previously mentioned the main purpose of a BA pool is to provide a trustworthy
verifiable mechanism for maintaining consistent updates to the distributed ledgor and database managed by that pool. These data
structures are designed such that a reader of a copy of the associated ledger or database can verify the consistency of the contents
via the included signarures. Because the majority of accesses by users to the ledger and database will be read not write, the relay.
network improves scalability by both balancing the read traffic across the relay hosts and reducing the read traffic on the BA
pool members. Because a relay node's copy of the ledgers and databases is read-only the only exploit a malicious relay node can
‘perform is to delete or ignore updates to the associated contents but not modify those contents. Thus any user merely necds to be
able to access one non-faulty relay node to be able to read and verify updates to the ledger and databases. This non-faulty relay
node could be under the control of the user or some other trusted entiy. Each user may select a sct of relay nodes to provide
higher availability. To restate, write updates require a trustworthy non-faulty majority. Whereas read verifies merely require a
trustworthy minority — as litle a5 one.

In addition to providing load balancing readiverify copies of the distributed ledgers and databascs, relay nodes may support
other functions. One of these is to provide a software defined data flow routing overlay [109]. This i similar to software defined
networking but decentralized [119]. This maps data flow addrosses to IP addresses. Thus a set of compute services or functions
can be pre-configured with data flow addresses that only need to be converted to the real TP addresses of the actual hosts at run
time. The relay nodes maintain a data flow route to [P routing table. This allows the serverless framework to dynamically
allocate compute resources without reprogramming or reconfiguration of compute services and functions.

Another funcion of the relay nefwark is to bootstrap peer-to-peer

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
communications befween nodes in the nefwork by
‘maintaining a DID-to-IP host:port mapping table. Most TP nodes in residential and commercial building networks do not have
public TP addresses but have only private P addresses and reside behind NAT routers. The default configuration of most NAT
routers enables dynamic port mapping (forwarding) [96]. Dynamic port mapping opens a retum port on the extemal (pubic) side
of the NAT router as a resul of an outgoing packet from the internal (private) side. This allows an internal host that does not
have a public IP address to initiate two-way communications with an external host that does have a public IP address. It does not
allow the converse. This is a problem for private hosts that want to work as compute servers. Private hosts can query the DID-to-
TP address table on relay network hosts that have public P addresses to helps bootstrap peer-to-peer commmunication with another
private host. The basic technique is called UDP hole punching [137). RAET is well suited to this approach. Hole punching is less
eliable with TCP connections [130]. n the event that hole punching does no work, two nodes may communicate using the relay
node as a repear.

‘Another function of the relay network is to provide a content and code delivery network (CDN). Client side web applications
require a static Ioad of content. Having this static content cached in nodes that are nearby the web clients reduces starfup delay
and minimizes bandwidth demands. Morcover pushing updates to compute nodes code and frameworks can induce spikes in
nefwork traffic. This is minimize with cached local copies. Video and audio streaming applications consume large amounts of
‘bandwidh this is also minimized if the content is cached locally.

Another function of a relay node is to facilitate federation of compute services. The relay nodes keep a local copy of the
federation groups for compute services. The relay nodes track also track presence, availability, and performance of any of their
conneeted or communicating nodes. This allows compute consumers to better dynamically select the specific servers to use from
their federation pool.

In general the relay network's purpose is to facilitate and improve performance, horizontal scaling and greater elasticity of the
computing infrastructure. The number of relay nodes is not fixed and may exceed the mumber of BA pool nodes by an order of
‘magnitude. The following notional diagram shows a BA pool with auxiliary r

analytics and anlysis that was the prime motivation for pursuing a deceniralized fog computing infrastructure in the first place.
The network effect benefis of IoT can best be realized when previously isolated o resources are enabled to interoperate. The
well know primary bariers 10 unleashing the beneficial network effects of 16T are concerns about securty, privacy, and agency.
Duc to & paucity of corresponding solutions, the foT market delivers value well below its potential. A solution that only
addresses one or two of these problematic barriers will be severely limited. Difuon provides a platform for federated ephemeral
edge computing that viably solves all three problems of sccurity, privacy, and agency.

Finally any private cloud application that requires fevels of privacy and security that are not easily attained in a public cloud
computing infrastructure might very well be betier suited for the Difuon public fog computing infrastructure. In order 10 be
decentralized und privato and sceurc requires that the public fog use a diffusc trust perimeter-ss sccurity model. This model
inherently provides mechanisns to enhance privacy, security, and control over applications and data whilsestill being hosted on
public compute infrastructure,

This approach s disruptive o the much more expensive cost basis of large centralized data-center hosted cloud services.
Difuon takes a truly comprehensive best practices approach to the transforming compute infrastructure from centralized siloed
one-sided networks to truly decentralized distributed two-sided netwrks. Only the latter can scale beneficially enabling the
broadest adoption and participation of both producers and consumers of compute services.

3. ConcLusion

‘The Difuon Fog exploits and integrates several emerging technologies to provide a decentralized computing infrastructure. for
Intemet of Things (IoT), Industrial Internet of Things (IloT), Intemet of Tiny Things (IoT), and personal data applications at
seale. The Difuon Fog provides a diffuse trust ephemeral computing frusted exeeution environment with end-to-end security (at
motion and at rest) and modular privacy (off-chain interaction) that runs a distributed serverless framework (FaaS). This is
essential enabling technology for a truly decentralized public fog computing

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
infrastructure.

The distributed nature of ToT means that processing and analysis scales better if aggregated in a bottom up hicrarchy from
Tocal to global. The data integration layer needs to be universal, not fragmented as it is now. It needs to leverage as much as
possible the existing cloud based tooling for distributed applications but adapted to the unique characteristis of the fog.

Given the rampant fragmentation, stratification and siloization of existing ToT device level protocols, frameworks, and
platforms, the only viable solution is an Intemet Protocol (IF) centric approach that enables reliable, secure, decentralized
integration across and through ToT silos and strata. This solution is a decentralized federated cphemeral edge computing
frameswork, platform, and marketplace for data integration and analysis. This s the Difuon Fog,

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
Tokenomics White Paper 2
order for a utility token to be attractive to investors it must have an appreciative quality, meaning that the investors must be able
{0 buy low and sell high. The hypothesis is that a large appreciative driver of price for the token can be created by limiting the
supply and making network function highly dependent on the use of the token [20; 41; 72]. As the value of transactions on the
network grows demand for transactions will drive demand for tokens which given the limited supply and functional dependency
will drve the price upwards. The token then scts s  sore of valu tha i appreciating over 7 9] The simple approch to
forcing functional dependency is to have a single utility token for all functions of the network. This also simplifies the software
‘needed 10 support the network function relative 1o the token. This is the approach that the majority of the carly and existing token
generating events have taken. A source of problem i the thought that ane should add as many sources of value o th token 5
possible in order to enhance the market value of the token [72].

“There are two scrious flave in this hypothesis. The frst s that mrcly lmiting the supply and functional dependency are ot
sufficient to drive the price upwards, the velocity of tumover of the token must also be kept low [4; 12; 15; 68]. That is, relative
10 the latency of transactions, the availability of token reuse for subsequent transactions must be kept low. This is in direct
conflict with the desiabilty of using a cypto-token a5 a ow ficion, o latency medium of exchangs. The second flaw is that
appreciative price of the token is in conflict with its use as a stable medium of exchange. As the token appreciates in price, the
price of producs and services on the network also increase making the network as a whole less atractve to alternative markets
that provide compeiive products and services{12; 15; 40]. This becomes a disincentive for participants o use the network to
exchange products and services. Only products whose demand is highly elastic relative to the price will be attractive on a
platform shere the pricing is going up fast elaive o the product ifecycle and opportunity cost elsewhers. Together the low
availability of tokens and the appreciative pricing are strong counter drivers to product demand. These means that the network

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
Tokenomics White Paper 4
of the single utiliy token hypothesis [35].
3. Mueri-UtiLity TOKENOMICS

A more complex nuanced approach is to use two or more tokens for the function of a given network. Given the fuilure of
single uility tokenomics, a motivation for a dual or multi-token approach is to support the separation of concerns between the
use ofa token as a medium-of-exchange (Mok) (non-appreciative, stable) and a store-of-value (SoV) (appreciative, investible).
Somewhat confusing is the fact that some networks like Ethereum (Cther and Gas) and VeChain (Vet and Thor) use dual uility
tokens to separate other concems such as a floating price relative to Fther in Gas for the spo pricing of computation on the
network independent of the pricing of products and services provided by the network [S1; 62; 67]. Such is an exanuple of dul
utilty tokens for two different network functions. Also confusing are dual tokens of different types. The first token is a utlity
oken, which is used as a medium-of-exchange on the platform. The other token could be of a different type such as a security
token or an asset backed token [1; 27]. A security token has rights to equity or share of profis and is not part of network function
but appreciates based on the vale of the equity andor profits generated by the nefwork [65: 66]. The problem with security
tokens is the lack of viable security token exchanges [5]. An asset backed token derives its value from an associated asset that
has intrinsic appreciative value based on property (real or intellectual) or a scarce commodity [3]. OF note is the distinction
between a tility token thatis sold as a security and a securty token. Under US securites law the Howie test determines whether
Something is a security or not. Simply if  token is sold that does not have true consumptive value o the buyer does not have
reasonable cansumptive intent then that token could be a security. Before  network or platform launches a medium-of-exchange
token has no consumptive value and is therefore its sale s likely to be considered that same as the issuance of a security [34; 42;
71]. Once the netwrk launches then sale of the token might not longer be considered a security issuance.

o that end, a better line of inquiry is: *Can a digital asset that was originally offered in a securities offering ever be
later sold in a manner that does not constitute an offering of a security?” In cases where the digital asset represents a
et of righs that gives the ho

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
lder a financial inferest in an enterprise, the answer is likely “no.” In these cases, calling
the transaction an inital coin offering. or “ICO.” or a sale of a “ioken,” will not take it out of the purview of the USS.
securities laws. But what about cases where there is no longer any ceniral enterprise being invested in or where the
digital asset is sold only 10 be used o purchase a good or service available through the network on which it was
created? I helieve in these cases the answer is a qualified “yes. "[71]

Herein we focus on multi-utliy tokenomics for the purpose of separating the two concems of medium-of-exchange and store-
of-value. The core thesis is that an appropriatly designed dual utily token model allows one of the tokens to be a storc-of-value
utilty token which provides liquidiy to investors when traded on available exchanges (unlike security tokens) and may
appreciate s a function of the valuc of the associated network while the other token acts as 4 low friction high velocity medium-
of-cxchange thus avoiding the drawbacks of a single utility token model. The key insight s that optional utiityis still tility. The
network can function as a successful compefitive business consummating transactions merely with a medium.-of-exchange utility
token without requiring a store-of-valuc uility token. However, the storc-of-value token, although optional, can provide
additional value to the participants in the network and to the function of the nefuwork that may be appreciative in nature [38]
Tndced. the flawed hypothesis that required uiiliy i cssctial forces the inherent conflct between appreciation, pricing clasticity,
and velocity that has resulted in the almost universal failure of single utility token models for anything other than a5 pump-and-
dump speculative vehicles,

‘The medium-of-cxchange token could be fiat. ndeed, given the low friction, relatively high stabilty, high availability and low
latency of fiat, it is difficult to argue that the medium-of-exchange should be anything ofher than fiat. Currently most non-fiat
tokens have super high friction to on-boarding participants and converting their fiat to non-fiat uility tokens. This friction has
become even higher in the USA, because the IRS classifies non-fiat crypto token exchanges as asset transactions subject to
capital gains taxcs. The biggest molivation for using & non-fiat token would be lower lateney and lower scitlement costs than
existing clectronic fiat payment channels. B

author: SmithSamuelM
content:
ut in cither case, because the world runs on fiat, having 2 transperent fiat payment
channel to access  non-fiat medium-of-cxchange token is essential to universal adoption of the network. One way to achieve
both stability and lower settlement costs is to peg the ulilty token to fiat for stability, but batch the conversion to and from fat to
reduce scttlement costs. The simplest pe is an cphemeral two-way one-to-onc peg with a liquidity pool in fiat. Ephemeral means
the token is minted and burmed on demand. A fial-pegged stable ephemral uility loken is nol appreciating or depreciating by
design o it is mot investable or tradab, but may only be used to acquired products and scrvices on a platform. These arc good
Teatures from a regulatory perspective. This type of ephemeral peg is in contradistinction (o non-cphemeral but sable crypto-
currencies that are meant (0 be traded [18].

An cxample of  utlit token medium-of-exchange with a fiat payment channel is the Stcllar nctwork, whercin fiat anchors
provide credit from fiat accounts for end-to-cnd fa-to-fiat transactions that use the utilty token (Lumen) as an ephemeral
‘medium-of-cxchange [56]. The fiat anchors provide fiat liquidity pools to back the credit they extend for the duration of the
transaction seutlement time (< 59). The result is that Stellar act as & transpareat, low [iction, low latency, fat payment channel
with low average scitlement costs. The Stronghold network on op of Stellar is providing a USD peged anchor [10. Utility
tokens used as & medium-of-exchange would want o exhibit similar features.

‘There are other more complex ways to implement a stable ulility token such as using an algorithun for fine wning dynamic
supply that may make the token even more stablc than fiat, However, for many two-sided network business modls, fiat is
sulficiently stable such that a simple peg i al that is needed if not fat isell
